offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing in Val Cenis is concentrated around the upper mountain and the Mont-Cenis plateau. North-facing couloirs above the main bowl hold snow well after storms. The treeline terrain offers glade-style riding reminiscent of North America, with powder pockets that stay untracked longer than in busier resorts. Marked itinerary routes provide a controlled introduction to unpisted terrain. For experienced skiers, local guides can access steeper couloirs and routes towards the Italian border. The proximity to the Vanoise National Park creates additional backcountry potential, though a guide is essential for safety. After fresh snowfall, the upper mountain remains untracked well into the morning due to low visitor numbers.
valueDescription: Val Cenis is one of the more affordable French resorts. During the 2023/24 season, a six-day lift pass cost €221, compared to €396 in Val d'Isère, €315 in Serre Chevalier, and €375 in the Three Valleys. Accommodation and meals are also reasonably priced, with a range of self-catering apartments and hotels. The resort offers a unique bad-weather insurance option: for an additional 8% of the lift pass cost, skiers can claim a refund for any day with three or more hours of rain or snow, while still using the pass that day. The Haute Maurienne Vanoise pass provides one free day at each of the other five resorts in the area, adding further value.
familyDescription: Val Cenis is well-suited to families. Both Lanslebourg and Lanslevillard have dedicated beginner areas at resort level, set away from faster traffic, with gentle nursery runs. The ESF runs children's programmes with snow gardens for young children. The Mickey run is a dedicated children's slope. The compact village scale means accommodation, slopes, and restaurants are within walking distance. Off the slopes, families can enjoy ice skating, sledging areas, and winter walking paths. The resort's sunny aspect means snow conditions soften during the day, making learning more forgiving. Older children and teenagers have blues and reds to explore, plus a terrain park.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Val Cenis is relaxed and low-key. Bars such as Le Chardon Bleu and Le C2 offer cozy spots for a drink. Dining options range from hearty Savoyard cuisine at Chalet La Ranôva to more sophisticated dishes at The Artemisia. The atmosphere is mellow rather than rowdy, with a handful of terraces and occasional live music. Reliable late-night bars are harder to find, especially mid-week. The resort is not a party destination, but there are enough options for a pleasant evening.
resortCharacterDescription: Val Cenis consists of three traditional Savoyard villages: Lanslebourg, Lanslevillard, and Termignon. The architecture features stone buildings and a genuine alpine character, with year-round communities rather than a purpose-built tourist development. The villages are uncommercialised, with a handful of good restaurants and a friendly, local atmosphere. The resort has been growing in popularity with British visitors seeking an alternative to the busier Tarentaise resorts. The historic Mont Cenis pass road to Italy starts just above Lanslebourg, adding to the frontier character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Val Cenis is modern and efficient, with high-speed chairlifts and gondolas connecting the three base villages. Multiple access points mean queues are rare. The lifts are comfortable and well-maintained, with minimal draglifts. The system allows easy navigation between the Lanslebourg and Lanslevillard sectors. Snowboarders may find some long flat sections challenging, but overall the lift network is well-designed for the terrain.
linkedResortsDescription: The Espace Haute Maurienne Vanoise comprises six ski resorts: Valfréjus, La Norma, Aussois, Val Cenis, Bessans, and Bonneval-sur-Arc. They are not connected by lifts but share a single ski pass. With a six-day or more pass for any resort, skiers get one free day at each of the other five resorts. The resorts are linked by free shuttle buses (lines S50-S53) that run between them and to Modane station. The total area offers 350km of downhill pistes and 200km of Nordic trails. Each resort has its own character: Val Cenis is the largest with varied terrain, Valfréjus is compact and family-friendly, Aussois is traditional with historic forts, La Norma is purpose-built for families, Bessans is a Nordic centre, and Bonneval-sur-Arc is a charming village known for freeride and ski touring.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Etna Nord (Piano Provenzana) consists of four main slopes served by lifts: one easy blue run on the Pouchoz–Tanaurpi chairlift (1,806–2,001 m, 1,302 m long) with a wide, gentle gradient suitable for beginners, and three expert red runs on the Monte Conca (1,817–2,093 m), Coccinelle (1,987–2,136 m), and Anfiteatro (2,056–2,338 m) ski lifts, which offer more technical descents with changing gradients. A tapis roulant is available for absolute beginners. The terrain is set on the northern slope of Mount Etna, surrounded by pine forests and ancient lava flows, with views of the Ionian Sea. The runs are natural and not heavily groomed, with volcanic rock occasionally peeking through the snow.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing on Etna Nord is possible on the volcanic terrain, with sections of ash and snow offering adventurous lines. The area retains a raw, mountain-oriented character, and experienced skiers can find untracked routes between the lava flows. However, conditions are highly variable due to volcanic activity, wind, and snow cover, and off-piste skiing is best undertaken with a certified alpine guide. Ski mountaineering is also popular, allowing access to higher elevations when lift-served terrain is limited.
valueDescription: A daily lift pass at Etna Nord costs approximately €35, and ski rental averages €40 for a full set. Accommodation in nearby Linguaglossa or Catania offers budget-friendly options, and the resort is generally less expensive than major Alpine resorts. The quieter northern side also means fewer crowds and lower costs for dining and services compared to the more touristy Etna Sud.
familyDescription: The resort offers a dedicated beginner area with a tapis roulant and a wide, gentle blue run on the Pouchoz–Tanaurpi chairlift, making it suitable for families with young children or beginners. Ski schools and equipment rental are available on site. The quieter northern side is less crowded than Etna Sud, providing a more relaxed environment for families. The surrounding pine forests and volcanic landscape also offer opportunities for snowshoeing and sledding.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski at Etna Nord is low-key, centered around the on-mountain restaurants and bars. Bar Ristorante La Vetta and Nino's Pub are popular spots for drinks and local Sicilian cuisine. The resort's chalets near the Pouchoz–Tanaurpi chairlift offer food and shelter, and the nearby town of Linguaglossa has additional dining options. The nightlife is quiet compared to larger resorts, with a focus on relaxed evenings and local wine.
resortCharacterDescription: Etna Nord (Piano Provenzana) is a natural, undeveloped ski area on the northern slope of Mount Etna, not a traditional resort village. Facilities are limited to a few chalets, a restaurant, and lift stations. The landscape is dominated by volcanic rock, pine forests, and panoramic views of the sea. It offers a raw, mountain-oriented experience with minimal commercial development, appealing to those seeking authenticity and solitude.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Etna Nord consists of one modern chairlift (Pouchoz–Tanaurpi) and three surface ski lifts (Monte Conca, Coccinelle, Anfiteatro), plus a tapis roulant for beginners. The lifts are generally well-maintained, though some may be older. The total uplift capacity is around 3,000 skiers per hour. Queues can occur on weekends, but the area is typically quiet during weekdays. Ticket purchase is streamlined with new pick-up boxes for online vouchers.
linkedResortsDescription: Etna Nord is a standalone ski area on the northern slope of Mount Etna, not physically connected to Etna Sud on the southern slope. The two areas are separate and require a road transfer of about 1 hour. There is no linked resort network; each side operates independently with its own lift system and services.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Feldis is dominated by intermediate red runs (7.8 km) with a smaller portion of advanced black runs (2.2 km) and no designated beginner blue runs. The slopes are oriented predominantly south (40%) and west (47%), with only 9% north-facing. The vertical drop is 522 m from the base at 1480 m to the summit at 1974 m. The chairlift to Mutta provides access to a variety of slopes offering panoramic views of Piz Beverin, Tödi, and the Calanda group. The pistes are clearly laid out and family-friendly, with a mix of open and tree-lined runs.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for adults costs CHF 35–39 depending on the season, and for children CHF 21. The resort is small with limited accommodation options, but self-catering apartments and budget-friendly hotels are available in the nearby village. The ski area is compact, so visitors can save on lift pass costs compared to larger resorts. No further details on value or budget-friendliness are provided in the source text.
familyDescription: Feldis is popular with families, offering a child-friendly chairlift and clearly laid out pistes that are easy for beginners and children. The resort features three toboggan runs and a natural ice rink on Alp Raguta for skating and curling. The small, uncrowded ski area and relaxed atmosphere make it suitable for leisurely skiing with children. Mountain restaurants like the Feldis ski hut serve local specialities and hot chocolate.
resortCharacterDescription: Feldis is a small, traditional Swiss village situated on a plateau above the Hinterrhein at about 1500 m. It has retained its original charm and offers panoramic views of the surrounding Alps, including Piz Beverin, Tödi, and the Calanda group. The resort is quiet and nature-oriented, away from mass tourism, with a focus on unspoilt nature and relaxation. In summer, it is a popular starting point for hikes and mountain tours.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises a cable car (Luftseilbahn) from Rhäzüns (663 m) to Feldis (1465 m), a chairlift (Sesselbahn) from Feldis to Mutta (1965 m), and two surface lifts. The cable car operates daily from 06:05 to 22:30 during the summer season, while the chairlift is seasonal. The system is small but adequate for the limited terrain, with a total of 4 lifts. No information on lift age or efficiency is provided.

terrainDescription: The terrain is on a sunny high plateau at 1,600 m, dominated by wide blue and red runs that are ideal for beginners and intermediates. There is a short black slope and a 6 km ungroomed ski route descending to Ebensee for advanced skiers. The slopes offer panoramic views of Lake Traunsee and the Dachstein mountains. The ski area covers 10–11 km of pistes, with 76% beginner, 17% intermediate, and 7% advanced runs.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is limited to a 6 km ungroomed descent from the summit to Ebensee, which is unsecured and requires good snow cover. There are also 4 marked ski touring routes and approximately 5 km of freeride terrain. The area is not a major off-piste destination, but the valley descent provides a challenging option for experienced skiers.
valueDescription: Lift passes are reasonably priced: a day ticket costs €59.50 for adults, with discounts for seniors and children born in 2020 or younger ski free with a parent. A 6-day pass is €312.10. Parking at the valley station is free, and a free ski bus (Traunsee Wintershuttle) is available. Accommodation options range from the on-mountain Berggasthof Edelweiss to hotels in Ebensee.
familyDescription: The resort is highly family-friendly with a dedicated children's area called Franzi's Dragonpark, featuring two conveyor belts, a rope lift, and playful features. The ski school at the mountain station offers courses for all levels. Children born in 2020 or younger ski free with a parent. The sunny plateau and gentle slopes make it a safe learning environment.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, centered around four mountain huts and restaurants on the plateau serving traditional Austrian dishes like Wiener Schnitzel and Kaiserschmarrn. Occasional torch-lit descents are offered. The village of Ebensee at the base has limited nightlife. The atmosphere is relaxed and family-oriented.
resortCharacterDescription: Feuerkogel is not a purpose-built resort village but a mountain plateau accessed by cable car from Ebensee. The plateau features a traditional mountain inn (Berggasthof Edelweiss) and several huts, offering a quiet, rustic alpine experience. The base town of Ebensee is a traditional Austrian town on Lake Traunsee.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a cable car from Ebensee to the plateau (10-minute ride, operating 8:30–17:00 daily), plus 7 additional lifts on the plateau including a high-speed six-pack chair and surface lifts. Total lift capacity is 8,720 persons per hour. Snowmaking covers the slopes. The lifts are modern and queues are rare.
linkedResortsDescription: Feuerkogel is part of the Kartenverbund Dachstein West, a multi-day ski pass that grants access to over 160 km of slopes across several ski areas in the Salzkammergut region, including Dachstein West, Dachstein Krippenstein, Karkogel, Sonnleitenlifte Abtenau, Skilift Lammertal Lungötz, St. Martin am Tennengebirge, and Zinkenlifte Bad Dürrnberg. This allows skiers to explore a wider variety of terrain beyond Feuerkogel's own 11 km.

terrainDescription: Flachau's terrain focuses on wide cruising runs through forests and open bowls above the treeline. The south-east facing slopes mean conditions soften early in the afternoon. Higher north-facing runs hold snow well, while lower slopes benefit from daily grooming and extensive snowmaking coverage.
offPisteDescription: Tree skiing through the forests below Grießenkareck offers gentle powder runs after snowfall, suitable for those new to off-piste. Serious off-piste enthusiasts will find the area limited, though guided tours can unlock more adventurous lines. The wider Ski Amadé area provides access to more serious freeride terrain for day trips.
familyDescription: Dedicated nursery areas sit right in the village, served by easy-access gondolas that keep beginners away from faster traffic. The ski school offers English-speaking group lessons for children from age two, with ski play lessons and children's ski courses from age four. There are eleven ski schools in the area, and facilities include snow kindergartens for the youngest visitors.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has a relaxed après-ski scene with traditional restaurants serving hearty regional specialities. At the valley stations, bars like 'Dampfkessel' and 'Hofstadl' offer food and drinks, while 'Downtown' and 'Ema's Pub' provide dancing. The après-ski options are all within accessible distance of the centre of Flachau.
resortCharacterDescription: Flachau is a valley village with traditional character, featuring church spires and wooden chalets. It sits at the heart of the Salzburger Sportwelt in the interconnected Ski Amadé area, retaining an authentic village atmosphere rather than a purpose-built resort feel.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of gondolas and high-speed chairlifts, with most chairlifts having weather protection bonnets, heated seats, and automatically closing safety bars for children. The G-Link cable car provides a fast connection between Flachau-Wagrain/Grießenkareck and Wagrain/Grafenberg-Alpendorf. Users rate lift efficiency highly, with minimal queuing time.
linkedResortsDescription: Flachau is part of the Snow Space Salzburg ski area, directly connected to Wagrain and St. Johann-Alpendorf, covering 210km of pistes. Through the Panorama Link gondola lift, it connects to the Shuttleberg Flachauwinkl-Kleinarl and Zauchensee-Flachauwinkl ski areas, totaling 210km of pistes. The wider Ski Amadé area unlocks 760km of pistes across multiple interconnected resorts.

terrainDescription: The Foppolo-Carona ski area offers 30 km of pistes served by 12 lifts, with a vertical drop from 2200m to 1635m. The Foppolo side features predominantly red and blue runs suitable for intermediate skiers, while the Carona side includes a black run on Monte Valgussera. The area is linked by piste, allowing skiers to explore both sides. Snowmaking covers the slopes to ensure reliable conditions.
valueDescription: A daily lift pass for the Foppolo-Carona area costs €40 for adults (festive rate). Accommodation is available in budget apartments and hotels, with self-catering options to save on dining. Restaurants offer pizzas for around €10 and beers for €3–4. Ski hire is cheap, around £80 for a week's snowboard rental. The resort is known as a budget-friendly destination, especially for day trips from Milan or Bergamo.
familyDescription: Foppolo is family-friendly with a dedicated ski school (Kinderpark Sci 90) offering 2-hour group lessons for children at €40. There is a sledging run in the via Foppelle area and a playground. The resort is quiet during weekdays, making it safe for beginners, but gets busier at weekends with day-trippers from Milan and Bergamo. Accommodation is close to slopes, and there is free parking.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, with a few restaurants and bars in the village. During the week, many establishments close early, but at weekends the atmosphere livens up with day visitors. There is a disco for evening entertainment. The resort is more suited to those seeking a quiet evening after skiing rather than a vibrant nightlife scene.
resortCharacterDescription: Foppolo is a traditional Italian mountain village in the Brembana Valley, with a mix of old stone buildings and modern apartment blocks. The village center has a few shops, restaurants, and a church. The infrastructure is somewhat dated, with many apartments showing their age, but the setting is scenic with views of the Orobie Alps. It is not a purpose-built resort but a genuine village that has grown around skiing.
liftSystemDescription: The Foppolo-Carona ski area has a total of 12 lifts, including chairlifts and surface lifts. The Foppolo side has 4 chairlifts, while the Carona side adds additional lifts. Some lifts are older and slower, but the network provides access to all slopes. The lifts are generally uncrowded on weekdays but can have queues on weekends.
linkedResortsDescription: Foppolo is linked by piste to the resort of Carona, forming the Brembo Ski area which also includes San Simone. A single ski pass gives access to all three resorts, with a total of 30 km of pistes and 12 lifts. The Carona side offers more challenging terrain, including a black run.

terrainDescription: The ski area is split into two zones connected by a cat-bus service. The lower Davost zone at valley level (around 900 m) has gentle slopes, moving walkways, ski lifts, and a race course, designed for beginners and children. The upper Varmost zone on Monte Crusicalas reaches 2,073 m and offers black and red pistes, including the longest slope in Friuli Venezia Giulia: a 6 km run through woods with views of the eastern Dolomites. The terrain is 71% intermediate, 26% beginner, and 3% advanced, with 13 km of pistes total.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are among the lowest in the region: an adult day pass costs €44, a youth (2007–2018) day pass €10, and children (2019–2023) ski free. A 6-day adult pass is €211. Accommodation includes over 4,000 beds in B&Bs, apartments, and hotels, with many self-catering options. The resort is described as a low-cost destination for families, with costs more contained than larger Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: The Davost zone has a dedicated nursery area with moving walkways and a race course, plus the Fantasy Snow Park with bobsleds, trampolines, and inflatables. An ice rink, indoor swimming pool, and wellness centre provide off-slope activities. Ski school offers lessons for children, and the village is compact and walkable. The resort is marketed as ideal for families with children learning to ski.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has 5 bars and pubs and 10 restaurants. Winter events include Christmas markets and a New Year's Eve torchlight procession. Summer features the Rock in For concert series. The atmosphere is quiet and family-oriented rather than party-focused.
resortCharacterDescription: Forni di Sopra is a traditional mountain village with stone and wood houses, set in the Friulian Dolomites Natural Park (UNESCO World Heritage). It consists of three hamlets: Vico, Andrazza, and Cella. The village has Roman origins and retains an authentic, unpurpose-built character. It is the most visited mountain settlement in Friuli Venezia Giulia, popular in both summer and winter.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 8 lifts: 3 quad chairs, 1 triple chair, and 4 surface lifts. All slopes are covered by artificial snowmaking, ensuring reliable conditions. The lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:00. The system is modern and well-maintained, with a capacity of 7,650 persons per hour.
linkedResortsDescription: The Forni di Sopra skipass is also valid at Piancavallo, Ravascletto-Zoncolan, Sappada, Sauris, Sella Nevea, and Tarvisio, allowing access to multiple ski areas in Friuli Venezia Giulia under a single pass. These resorts are not physically connected by lifts but are within driving distance.

terrainDescription: The resort offers FISI-approved slopes, with a mix of blue and green runs suitable for beginners and intermediates. The Malanotte downhill run is equipped with snow-making. The longest run is 5 km. A dedicated children's area, Frabolandia, features inflatables, trampolines, and bobsleighs.
familyDescription: Frabosa Soprana has dedicated blue and green slopes for children, and the Frabolandia Snow Park offers inflatables, trampolines, a ball pool, playhouses, slides, and bobsleighs. The resort provides personalised services for families and has a wellness centre and solarium areas.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include mountain chalets serving traditional local dishes, particularly Raschera DOP cheese. The village also has bars and restaurants.
resortCharacterDescription: Frabosa Soprana is a traditional Italian mountain village that has been a tourist resort since the late 1800s. The old village centre retains its characteristic architecture with narrow streets and stone buildings. The resort offers a warm, welcoming atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 7 lifts, comprising chairlifts and surface lifts. The Monte Moro chairlift, inaugurated in 1948, was one of the first in Italy. A chairlift connects Frabosa Soprana to the Prato Nevoso ski resort, providing access to the wider Mondolè Ski area.
linkedResortsDescription: Frabosa Soprana is connected via chairlift to Prato Nevoso, and together with Artesina forms the Mondolè Ski area. The combined area offers over 100 km of slopes and 30 lifts.

terrainDescription: The resort has 11 km of groomed pistes, consisting of 2 km of blue runs and 9 km of red runs. There are no black runs. The main attraction is the 9 km valley run from the summit to Obertraun, which is the longest downhill run in Upper Austria with 1,500 meters of vertical descent. The terrain is high alpine and somewhat rough, with wide, scenic slopes. A short chairlift near the summit serves beginner terrain, and Krippi Park at the valley station offers a gentle learning area with a magic carpet.
offPisteDescription: Krippenstein is a freerider's paradise with over 30 km of marked off-piste variants. The terrain includes rough rocky ridges, varied tracks, and selective forest routes in areas like Schönberg, Imisl, and Eisgrube, offering 1,500 meters of altitude per ride. Powder-filled descents, open bowls, and tree runs provide backcountry-style skiing. The resort hosts avalanche safety training and guided freeride tours through the FreeSports Arena, with courses for all levels: Freeride Easy, Freeride Amateur, and Freeride Pro.
familyDescription: Krippi Park at the valley station has a magic carpet and T-bar lift for beginners, providing a safe learning area for children. The ski school offers private courses for beginners and advanced skiers. There is a family-friendly slope near Gjaidalm at the middle station, and the resort is described as laid-back and family-friendly. The compact layout and short walks from accommodation to lifts make it convenient for families.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a small, off-the-beaten-path ski area above the village of Obertraun, part of the UNESCO World Heritage region Salzkammergut. It has a tranquil, laid-back atmosphere far from mainstream crowds. The base village Obertraun is a peaceful lakeside village at 512 meters. The mountain station at 2,100 meters offers panoramic views of Hallstatt and the Dachstein glacier. The resort emphasizes low-impact tourism and sustainability, with all lift systems powered by renewable energy.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts: 3 gondolas/trams, 1 quad chair, and 2 surface lifts. The Dachstein Krippenstein cable car has two sections from Obertraun to the summit, operating every 15 minutes from 08:30 to 16:50 (lift hours 9:00–16:00). The cable cars are described as ultramodern and are supplied with electricity from renewable energy sources. A short chairlift near the summit serves beginner terrain.
linkedResortsDescription: With a multi-day ski pass of 1.5 days or more, skiers can access the wider Dachstein West ski region, which includes Dachstein West (Gosau, Russbach, Annaberg), Feuerkogel Ebensee, Karkogel & Sonnleitenlifte Abtenau, Skilift Lammertal Lungötz, St. Martin am Tennengebirge, Zinkenlifte Bad Dürrnberg, and Werfenweng (with season pass). This gives access to over 160 km of slopes and 70 lifts. The Dachstein West season pass also includes these areas.

terrainDescription: The Galibier-Thabor ski area is spread over three linked sectors: Les Vernays and Setaz to the south, and Crey du Quart to the east, the latter linking with Valmeinier. The lower slopes of Thimel mountain in the Setaz sector are wooded, offering tree-lined runs back to the village. Most terrain sits on open, rolling ridges between Valloire and Valmeinier, with the majority of pistes being intermediate reds. There are a handful of black runs, including two on the Grand Plateau (Cotérieux and Méregers) and two at the Cornafond chairlift. Green and blue runs make up over half the domain, with beginner zones at altitude in both main sectors. An easy circuit of narrow, zigzagging trails links all sectors.
offPisteDescription: The best off-piste opportunities are in the Crey du Quart sector on the Valloire side and Le Gros Crey on the Valmeinier side. A steep off-piste descent from the Armera piste above the treeline drops onto the blue Grapil piste. After fresh snowfall, powder can be found around Les Verneys. Black runs Mérégers, Dahu, Chamois, and Edelweiss are normally left ungroomed, developing mogul fields. A guide is recommended for exploring off-piste terrain.
valueDescription: Lift passes are moderately priced: a six-day adult pass costs €257.10 in low season and a day pass is €53.60 (2025/26 prices). The resort is less well-known than mainstream French resorts, and accommodation and dining are generally affordable. Self-catering apartments and mid-range hotels are common. The resort is popular with school groups seeking good value.
familyDescription: Valloire has a free trackless mini-train connecting lift terminals and accommodation, plus a free shuttle bus to outlying hamlets. The VALKID space offers a dedicated fun area for children. There are multiple beginner zones, including a compact area at Les Vernays with a free ski-tow, and additional nursery slopes at altitude. Off-slope activities include a full-size outdoor ice rink, a ten-pin bowling alley (open until 2am), and a cinema. The village is flat and easy to walk around.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is subdued, centred on relaxed local bars such as Pub L’Atelier (with occasional live music) and Le Bar à Zing. Most evening entertainment revolves around leisurely dinners in traditional Savoyard restaurants. Additional options include the outdoor ice rink, a ten-pin bowling alley (licensed until 2am), and a cinema. In mid-to-late January, an international ice and snow-sculpture competition decorates the village streets.
resortCharacterDescription: Valloire is a traditional Alpine village centred around a 17th-century Baroque church, with narrow old-town streets at its core and wider avenues on the periphery. The valley-floor location makes it flat and easy to stroll. The community includes 17 outlying hamlets. The atmosphere is friendly and relaxed, with a traditional French ambience. Valmeinier, the linked resort, is more purpose-built with modern apartment blocks.
liftSystemDescription: The ski area has 30 lifts: 2 gondolas (cable cars), 17 chairlifts, 10 drag lifts, and 1 carpet lift. The two main access points from Valloire are gondolas at the southern and eastern edges of the village, serving the Setaz and Crey du Quart sectors. Two roadside chairlifts at Les Clots and Les Vernays provide additional access to Setaz. Four lifts are accessible to pedestrians. The lift system is rated 6/10 for efficiency.
linkedResortsDescription: The Galibier-Thabor ski area links the resorts of Valloire and Valmeinier, forming a single domain of 160 km of pistes. The two resorts are connected by slopes and lifts, with a single ski pass valid across the entire area. Valloire is a traditional Alpine village, while Valmeinier is a more purpose-built resort. The linked area covers three mountains: Le Gros Crey, Le Grand Plateau, and La Setaz des Pres.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of 15.5 km of groomed pistes, with 7 km easy (blue), 7 km intermediate (red), and 1.5 km difficult (black). The black run, Direttissima Terzerhaus, is the steepest natural piste in Lower Austria with a 74% gradient. A fun slope with steep curves, waves, and sound effects is located at the valley station, and a speed track with time measurement is also present. The summit area offers freeride terrain. The slopes are served by two modern chairlifts (Bodenbauer-Express 4-seater and Gipfelbahn 2-seater) and a drag lift (Birkischlepper).
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available in the summit area, with designated freeride zones. Two signposted ski touring routes of varying difficulty are marked, and an avalanche transceiver checkpoint is provided at the access area. The Snowpark Gemeindealpe offers natural terrain with kickers for freestyle. The freeride area below the summit is noted for deep snow conditions.
valueDescription: A day ticket costs €50.50 for adults and €26.50 for children. A 6-day pass is €234.50 for adults and €122 for children. A season ticket is available for €299 for adults and €157 for children with the NÖ Card. Discounts include the Mountain Adventure Pass, guest cards, and a senior card for ages 61 and over. A combined ticket with a return train ride from St. Pölten is also offered. The ski pass is valid across the linked resorts of Mariazell and Annaberg. Holders of a valid ski pass can travel free on the Mariazellerbahn between Mitterbach and Mariazell.
familyDescription: The resort offers a magic carpet for beginners, used exclusively by the ski school. A fun slope with waves and sound effects provides entertainment for children. The snow park has natural terrain features. A 4.6 km toboggan run is available. In summer, an 800 m pushchair-accessible panoramic trail circles the summit. The Terzerhaus mountain restaurant has an adventure climbing playground and a giant swing. The ski school offers lessons for all ages.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski options include the Terzerhaus mountain restaurant at the summit, offering regional dishes and panoramic views. The s'Balzplatzerl at the middle station and the Zum Bäreneck ski hut also serve food and drinks. At the valley station, the Zum Sessellift inn provides refreshments. On Wednesdays, the slopes are open until 7 p.m. for night skiing. The scene is relaxed and family-oriented rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Mitterbach is a small village located in the Ötscher-Tormäuer Nature Park, the largest nature reserve in Lower Austria. The resort is not purpose-built; it has a traditional alpine character with rustic huts and inns. The lift infrastructure is modern, with two chairlifts and a drag lift, but the village retains its charm. The area is also a Natura 2000 protected site, home to black grouse, and the resort closes for six weeks in spring to protect wildlife.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of four lifts: the Bodenbauer-Express 4-seater chairlift (800 m to 1,300 m, 10 min ride), the Gipfelbahn 2-seater chairlift (1,300 m to 1,626 m, 7 min in winter), the Birkischlepper drag lift (637 m, 4 min), and the Grabnerlift drag lift (176 m, 1.5 min). A magic carpet (15 m) is used by the ski school. The total uphill capacity is 3,800 people per hour. The lifts are modern, with the chairlifts built in 2003 and the drag lift added in 2013. A new piste guidance system with panoramic boards was installed.
linkedResortsDescription: The Gemeindealpe Mitterbach ski pass is valid across the linked resorts of Mariazell (Bürgeralpe) and Annaberg, allowing access to all three ski areas with a single pass. This provides a combined ski area with additional terrain and lifts.

terrainDescription: The ski terrain at Glacier 3000 is split between a high-altitude glacier plateau and steep descents to the valley. On the summit glacier at 2,971 m, five well-groomed wide runs are served by three long T-bars, offering easy intermediate cruising. Below the plateau, a quad chair gives access to a single prepared red run (the Red Run) that descends 8 km with 1,700 m vertical drop. A 265 m tunnel, opened in 2023, leads to a 3 km black run (the Black Wall) with pitches up to 41 degrees and a 1,000 m+ vertical descent to Col du Pillon at 1,546 m. The Combe d'Audon is a 7 km black run with 1,137 m vertical. All runs are above treeline on the glacier, with no tree cover.
offPisteDescription: Glacier 3000 is renowned for extreme freeride terrain. From the Scex Rouge summit, off-piste descents drop precipitously over the face under the cable car, with cliffs, chutes, and a 102% (46°) pitch on the Black Wall. The off-piste area includes hundreds of acres of steep terrain, often requiring a guide. Lift-accessible sidecountry is available, with natural features like cliffs and kickers. The freeride itineraries are marked and can hold powder for several days after a storm. The terrain is exposed and high-consequence, suitable for expert skiers.
valueDescription: A day lift pass at Glacier 3000 costs CHF 79 (2025) to CHF 89 (2026) for adults, CHF 71 for youth/seniors, and CHF 45–51 for children. A six-day pass is CHF 340, and a season pass is CHF 499. The Magic Pass (CHF 499) includes Glacier 3000 plus many other resorts in the region, such as Villars-Gryon-Diablerets, Leysin, and others. There is no accommodation at the base; visitors stay in Les Diablerets or Gstaad, where lodging ranges from budget hostels to luxury hotels. The cable car round-trip for pedestrians is CHF 89 adult / CHF 45 child, with 50% discounts for Swiss Travel Pass holders.
familyDescription: Childcare facilities at Glacier 3000 are limited (rated 2.0 out of 5). The beginner terrain consists of a few gentle slopes on the glacier, suitable for skiers who already know the basics; first-timers are better served at nearby Villars. The resort has a snowpark and a fun park with sledging, but no dedicated beginner area at the base. Children under 6 travel free on the cable car. Ski schools are available with multilingual instructors (rated 4.0). Accommodation is not at the resort, so families must commute from Les Diablerets or Gstaad via bus or car.
resortCharacterDescription: Glacier 3000 is a high-altitude ski area, not a traditional village resort. The base is at Col du Pillon, a cable car station with a restaurant and free parking. The summit station at Scex Rouge (2,971 m) is a modern glass-and-metal building designed by Swiss architect Mario Botta, featuring a panoramic roof terrace, a self-service restaurant (Le 3), a table-service restaurant (Botta), and a chalet-style eatery (Le Carnotzet). There is no accommodation or nightlife at the resort itself; the nearest villages are Les Diablerets (15 minutes by bus) and Gstaad (30 minutes by bus), which offer traditional Swiss alpine charm.
liftSystemDescription: Access to Glacier 3000 is via a two-stage cable car from Col du Pillon (1,546 m) to the mid-station Cabane des Diablerets, then to the summit Scex Rouge (2,971 m) – total journey 15 minutes. On the glacier, three long T-bars serve the upper runs, and a quad chair serves the red run. A removable 150 m Sunkid Mega Comfort Star surface tow was installed in 2021 to access the Combe d'Audon black run; it can be raised to 5 m for night grooming. A 265 m tunnel through the mountain, opened in 2023, provides access to the Black Wall run. The lift system includes 3 gondolas/cable cars, 3 chairlifts, and 4 surface lifts (total 10 lifts per some sources). The old chairlift shared with foot passengers is slow but uncrowded.
linkedResortsDescription: Glacier 3000 is part of the Magic Pass network, which includes the linked resorts of Les Diablerets, Villars, Gryon, Leysin, Vercorin, Grimentz, Zinal, St-Luc/Chandolin, Leukerbad, Les Marecottes, Nax Mont Noble, Anzère, Saas Fee, and many others. It is also connected to the Gstaad Super Ski Region (Gstaad, Zweisimmen, Saanenmöser) via a free bus or short drive. The same lift pass (Magic Pass) grants access to all these areas, providing a wide variety of terrain and snow conditions.

terrainDescription: The ski terrain at Golte is spread across a high plateau between 1,280m and 1,576m, covering approximately 50 hectares. The slopes are wide and offer views of the Kamnik-Savinja Alps. The run network includes a mix of blue, red, and black pistes, with a dedicated children's polygon equipped with a conveyor belt. The longest run is 1.6 km. The resort has 11 named slopes, including Medvedjak, Limona, Poljana, and Družinska, with grooming provided daily to maintain consistent conditions.
familyDescription: Golte is well suited for families, with a dedicated children's polygon and a conveyor belt for beginners. The 'White Rabbit' ski school employs 25 instructors and has been active at the resort since 2005, offering group and individual lessons for children and adults. A kindergarten in the snow is available, and the Morava drag lift provides easy access for young skiers to practice. The resort also offers affordable day skiing for beginners, and the compact layout means accommodation and ski school are within walking distance.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski at Golte is low-key. The resort has a new après-ski stop called Trije Ploti hut, opened in January 2026. Bar Ledenica operates late into the night for hotel guests and external visitors, especially during New Year's Eve celebrations. The main restaurant Medvedjak has a terrace for mountain dining. The scene is relaxed rather than lively, with most evening activity centred around the hotel Montis and its wellness facilities.
resortCharacterDescription: Golte is a natural high plateau on the eastern edge of the Kamnik-Savinja Alps, not a purpose-built resort village. The landscape park setting offers skiing 'above the clouds' with panoramic views. The main accommodation is the Wellness Hotel Montis****, which includes a wellness centre, restaurant, and fitness facilities. Additional apartments are available nearby. The resort has a traditional Slovenian alpine character, with spruce forests and a calm atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a cable car (the longest in Slovenia, with an 8-minute ride from Žekovec to the plateau), three chairlifts (Smrekovec, Medvedjak, Ročka), two surface lifts (Morava, Stari Stani), and a conveyor belt for the children's area. The cable car operates on weekends from 8:00 to 18:00, every full hour. During the 2025/26 season, the lifts were not operating at the time of the source text, but the resort typically opens from late December to March.

terrainDescription: Graukogel's terrain is concentrated on a single forested mountain with 14 km of pistes, mostly wind-protected by the trees. The slopes are predominantly intermediate (63%) with a long red run and a challenging black run, the 2 km Toni-Sailer-Strecke. Most runs are not artificially snowed, relying on natural snow. The vertical drop is 928 m from summit (2,007 m) to base (1,079 m). The area has 3 lifts: two double chairlifts and one t-bar.
offPisteDescription: Graukogel offers two black runs, one of which is left ungroomed after snowfall, providing powder skiing for advanced skiers. The forested slopes allow for tree skiing. Ski touring routes, including the Zirbenroas, start from the area. The natural snow cover (no artificial snow) means off-piste conditions depend on recent snowfall. The area is known for its tranquil, natural setting with ancient pine forests.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass at Graukogel costs €55, with youth passes at €43 and child passes at €28. From 1.5 days onward, ski passes are valid across the entire Ski Amadé network, which includes over 200 km of pistes in the Gastein Valley. Accommodation and dining in Bad Gastein are typical for Austrian resorts, with self-catering apartments and family-run hotels available. The area is less crowded than the main Stubnerkogel-Schlossalm circuit, offering good value for those seeking quiet slopes.
familyDescription: Graukogel is not particularly family-friendly; its terrain is predominantly challenging (63% intermediate, 13% advanced) with long, steep runs and no dedicated beginner area. The 25% beginner terrain consists of a short blue run near the base, but nursery slopes and ski school meeting points are located in the main Bad Gastein area (Stubnerkogel) rather than at Graukogel. Families with young children would be better served by the Schlossalm-Angertal area, which has gentle slopes and children's facilities.
apresSkiDescription: Graukogel has no apres-ski facilities on the mountain; the two mountain huts (Graukogelalm at 1,460 m and Graukogelhütte at 1,954 m) serve food and drink during operating hours but close by 16:00. Evening entertainment is in Bad Gastein, a 5-minute drive or ski bus ride away, where the Silver Bullet Bar, casino, and Felsentherme thermal baths are located. The wider Gastein Valley offers a relaxed apres-ski scene focused on dining and hotel bars rather than loud parties.
resortCharacterDescription: Graukogel is a ski area on a forested mountain above Bad Gastein, a historic spa town with belle époque architecture and a dramatic gorge setting. The area itself is natural and tranquil, with ancient pine forests and no purpose-built village. The base station is at the edge of Bad Gastein, which features grand hotels, a frozen waterfall, and thermal springs. The character is traditional Austrian, with a quiet, uncrowded atmosphere compared to the main Stubnerkogel-Schlossalm area.
liftSystemDescription: Graukogel's lift system consists of two double chairlifts and one t-bar, with a total capacity of 3,348 persons per hour. The lifts are not high-speed; the double chairs are older models. Operating hours are 08:30 to 16:00. There is no gondola or cable car. The lifts are not connected to other ski areas; a ski bus is required to reach the main Stubnerkogel-Schlossalm area. Lift queues are minimal due to the small size of the area.
linkedResortsDescription: Graukogel is not directly lift-linked to other ski areas. A ski bus connects to the Stubnerkogel-Schlossalm-Angertal area (86 km of pistes) and to Sportgastein and Dorfgastein-Großarltal. Multi-day Ski Amadé passes (from 1.5 days) provide access to the entire Ski Amadé network, which includes over 200 km of pistes in the Gastein Valley. The main Bad Gastein area (Stubnerkogel) is linked to Bad Hofgastein's Schlossalm area, forming a larger interconnected domain.

terrainDescription: The alpine ski area of Gresse-en-Vercors stretches from 1,250m to 1,750m and is divided into two sectors: Les Alleyrons and Pierre Blanche. The runs are predominantly forested, with 9 green, 6 blue, 8 red, and 3 black runs according to the local tourism office. There are also three boarder cross courses and a snow carpet. The longest run is 4.5 km. Over fifty snow cannons cover the beginner areas to supplement natural snowfall.
valueDescription: A six-day adult lift pass costs between €76.20 (low season) and €106.60 (high season) according to Snowplaza, while Skidata reports €108 for a six-day pass. Self-catering studios start at €320 per week during school holidays, and a four-person apartment at €500. Ski equipment rental is €90 per week for adults and €40 for children. The resort is noted as one of the most economical in the region.
familyDescription: The resort has two large snow fronts with a dedicated beginner area and a toboggan run. The ESF ski school offers lessons from age 4 in a secure snow garden equipped with a magic carpet. Childcare is available during vacation periods. The village is compact and walkable, with a nursery, free ski storage, and a doctor's office. The slopes are mainly forested, providing shelter from wind.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has no bars or discos. There are six restaurants and six cafés offering regional Dauphinois cuisine. A cinema and a sauna with steam room are available for evening relaxation.
resortCharacterDescription: Gresse-en-Vercors is a traditional high-mountain village at 1,200m, the highest in the Vercors. The village features old stone houses, a 13th-century church dedicated to St. Bartholomew, and a population of around 380. It is located within the Vercors Regional Natural Park, offering scenic surroundings of cliffs, meadows, and forests. The resort facilities are at 1,250m, facing the Grand Veymont rock face.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 11 lifts: one fixed-grip chairlift, nine surface lifts, and one magic carpet. There is a free 'Transtation' shuttle within the resort. Over fifty snow cannons cover the beginner areas. The chairlift can occasionally close due to technical issues.

terrainDescription: The terrain spans 213 km of pistes across 55 runs (16 blue, 24 red, 15 black) with a vertical drop of 1,900 m. The main area around Kleine Scheidegg and Männlichen offers wide, cruisy intermediate runs with spectacular views of the Eiger, Mönch, and Jungfrau. The Lauberhorn World Cup downhill run is open to the public after the race, providing a challenging red/black run. The Grindelwald-First side is more playful with a snowpark and freestyle features. Advanced skiers can find steep pitches on the Black Rock piste and off-piste routes in the trees.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is available in the trees around Kleine Scheidegg and on the steeper faces below the Eiger. A local guide is recommended due to avalanche risk. The snow can stay fresh for a day or two after a storm, especially on north-facing slopes. The area is not known for extreme off-piste compared to resorts like Verbier, but there are good tree runs and some couloirs accessible with hiking.
valueDescription: Grindelwald is generally expensive, with lift passes around CHF 79 per day (2025/26) and high costs for food and accommodation. However, budget options exist: self-catering apartments and cheaper hotels are available, and the Interdiscount promotion offered passes for CHF 49. The Swiss Half Fare Card and regional passes can reduce costs. Overall, it is not a budget destination.
familyDescription: Families are well catered for with the bodmiARENA in Grindelwald village, a dedicated beginner area with conveyor belts and a platter lift. The Männlichen children's paradise at 2,232 m offers ski school for young children. The car-free village of Wengen provides a safe environment. The ski school is highly rated (4.58/5) with multilingual instructors. Children under 6 ski free, and on Saturdays up to three children (6-15) ski free with a paying adult.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Grindelwald is relaxed rather than rowdy. The village has several bars and pubs, including the popular 'Start Bar' and 'Lauberhorn' bar. Wengen offers a quieter scene with traditional fondue restaurants. Nightlife is limited compared to large French resorts, but there are a few clubs open late. The mountain restaurants like Skibar Läger on Männlichen offer afternoon drinks with views.
resortCharacterDescription: Grindelwald is a traditional Swiss mountain village with a mix of historic chalets and modern infrastructure. The village centre has a main street with shops, bakeries, and hotels. The Grindelwald Terminal is a modern transport hub with a shopping centre. Wengen is car-free and more traditional. The setting is spectacular, with the Eiger North Face looming overhead.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system has seen significant investment, notably the Eiger Express 3S gondola (opened 2020) which moves 2,400 people per hour from Grindelwald Terminal to Eigergletscher in 15 minutes. The Männlichenbahn is a 10-person gondola. In total, the area has around 40 lifts including gondolas, chairlifts, and surface lifts. Queues are rare outside peak weeks. The cog railway from Kleine Scheidegg to Jungfraujoch is a separate ticket.
linkedResortsDescription: Grindelwald is part of the Jungfrau Ski Region, which includes four interconnected ski areas: Grindelwald-Wengen (Kleine Scheidegg/Männlichen), Grindelwald-First, Mürren-Schilthorn, and Meiringen-Hasliberg. The total skiable terrain is over 250 km. The areas are linked by trains, gondolas, and buses. A single ski pass gives access to all areas. The Jungfraujoch – Top of Europe is accessible via cog railway from Kleine Scheidegg (separate ticket).

terrainDescription: The Grindelwald-Wengen ski area (Kleine Scheidegg-Männlichen) is dominated by wide, cruisy intermediate pistes, with long red and blue runs descending from Kleine Scheidegg and Männlichen towards Grindelwald or Wengen. Notable runs include the Lauberhorn (red), the longest World Cup downhill run at 4.455 km, with fast straightaways and wide curves; the Eiger Run (red) winding below the Eiger cliffs; and Black Rock (black) with sharp inclines. The Bodmi Arena at village level offers a dedicated beginner area with conveyor belts and a platter lift. The terrain includes 32% beginner, 42% intermediate, and 27% advanced slopes, with tree runs and open sections. The area is split between the Männlichen and Kleine Scheidegg sectors, both offering views of the Eiger, Mönch, and Jungfrau.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste opportunities exist but are more limited than in some Swiss resorts. Guided freeride tours are available in the wider Jungfrau Region, including around Schilthorn. Some tree runs in deep powder can be found, but avalanche risk is real and conditions change quickly; a local guide is recommended. The area is not known for extensive off-piste terrain, and most skiers stick to groomed runs.
valueDescription: Lift passes are expensive: a one-day adult pass costs approximately CHF 75, and a six-day pass around CHF 370. Children aged 6–15 receive about 50% reduction, and under-6s ski free. Accommodation and food are also costly, with many visitors noting the high overall expense. Best value periods are early January (after New Year) and March, when prices are lower and queues shorter. Self-catering apartments and guesthouses offer more budget-friendly lodging options.
familyDescription: Wengen is a car-free village, creating a safe, traffic-free environment for families. The Bodmi Arena in Grindelwald provides a dedicated beginner area with conveyor belts and platter lifts, ideal for first-timers. Ski schools are highly rated (4.58/5 on Snomad) and offer multi-language instruction. The Männlichen children’s paradise at 2,232 m has a playful learning area. Non-skiing activities include sledging (e.g., the 4.5 km Fox Run, the Jungfrau Eiger Run), the First Cliff Walk, and winter hiking trails. The village has supermarkets, bakeries, and easy logistics for families.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Grindelwald and Wengen is sociable and relaxed rather than rowdy. Popular venues include the Avocado Bar, Mescalero Club, Start Bar, and the Lauberhorn bar. Most visitors enjoy long dinners or drinks in cosy hotel lounges. The scene is more about quality than quantity, with a few late-night options but no table-dancing or large clubs. Wengen has a quieter, more traditional atmosphere, while Grindelwald offers a few more lively spots.
resortCharacterDescription: Grindelwald is a traditional Swiss alpine village with wooden chalets, a proper main street with supermarkets and bakeries, and a church steeple. It is not purpose-built; it has grown organically as a farming and tourism community. Wengen is a car-free mountain village at 1,274 m, with traditional hotels and a quiet, postcard-pretty character. Both villages retain authentic charm, with the dramatic peaks of the Eiger, Mönch, and Jungfrau rising above.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system is modern and efficient, with significant recent investment. The Eiger Express tri-cable gondola links Grindelwald Terminal directly to Eigergletscher, cutting access time to the upper slopes. The V-Cableway from Grund to Männlichen is a new 10-passenger gondola. The Wengen-Männlichen aerial cableway takes 10 minutes, and the Wengernalp Railway cog train runs from Wengen to Kleine Scheidegg in 25 minutes. The area has 27 lifts including chairlifts, surface lifts, and gondolas. Queues are rare outside peak weeks, and the system is well-integrated with trains and cable cars.
linkedResortsDescription: The Grindelwald-Wengen ski area is part of the larger Jungfrau Ski Region, which also includes Grindelwald-First (56 km of pistes, freestyle-focused), Mürren-Schilthorn (56 km, highest skiing in the Bernese Oberland up to 2,970 m), and Meiringen-Hasliberg (60 km). The areas are connected by lifts, cog railways, and cable cars: the Eiger Express links Grindelwald to Eigergletscher, from where skiers can reach Kleine Scheidegg and Männlichen; the Wengernalp Railway connects Wengen to Kleine Scheidegg; and the Männlichen cable car links to Wengen. The entire Jungfrau Ski Region offers over 200 km of groomed pistes across four sectors, with a single ski pass covering all areas.

terrainDescription: The Grindelwald ski area spans 213 km of marked pistes across two main sectors: Grindelwald-Wengen (linked via Kleine Scheidegg) and Grindelwald-First. The terrain includes 16 blue, 24 red, and 15 black runs, with a vertical drop of 1,900 m from the highest lift at 2,950 m down to the village at 1,050 m. The Grindelwald-Wengen side features long, well-groomed red runs such as the Lauberhorn World Cup course (4.5 km) and the Eiger Run, while the First sector offers gentler blues and a snowpark. The Bodmi nursery area at the base provides wide, gentle slopes for beginners. The Eiger Express gondola, completed in 2020, connects the village to the Eigergletscher station at 2,320 m in 15 minutes, opening up the upper mountain.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain in the Grindelwald area is concentrated beneath the Eiger's north face and around the Kleine Scheidegg region. The slopes are glaciated and committing, requiring a guide and avalanche equipment. The marked itinerary routes (not patrolled) include the Vallon d'Arbi and Gentianes on the Verbier side, but for Grindelwald specifically, the off-piste is accessed via the Eigergletscher and the area around the Mönch. The terrain is technical and snow conditions vary; a local guide is essential for safe exploration.
familyDescription: Grindelwald has a dedicated nursery area at Bodmi, with wide, gentle slopes for first-timers. The blues around Kleine Scheidegg are scenic and suitable for progressing beginners. The Männlichen children's paradise at 2,232 m offers ski lessons for young children. On Saturdays, up to three children (6-15 years) ski free with an adult buying a day pass. The village is car-free in parts, but Grindelwald itself has traffic; however, the car-free villages of Wengen and Mürren are nearby. The Bodmi area is centrally located and easily accessible from the village.
resortCharacterDescription: Grindelwald is a traditional Swiss village with a history dating back to 1146, set in a broad valley at 1,034 m beneath the Eiger's north face. The village has a mix of historic chalets and modern hotels, with a pedestrian-friendly main street. The recent Eiger Express gondola (2020) and the V-cableway have added modern infrastructure, but the resort retains an alpine character. The village is not car-free, but the nearby Wengen and Mürren are car-free. The surrounding landscape is dominated by glaciers and peaks, and the village is part of the Jungfrau-Aletsch UNESCO World Heritage site.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Grindelwald includes the Eiger Express, a tricable gondola that connects the village terminal to Eigergletscher (2,320 m) in 15 minutes, completed in 2020. The Grindelwald-First gondola is a 6-seater that takes 25 minutes to the top at 2,168 m, with mid-stations at Bort and Schreckfeld. The Grindelwald-Wengen area is served by the Wengernalp Railway (cog railway) to Kleine Scheidegg, and the Männlichen cable car. In total, the Grindelwald area has 20 lifts, including chairlifts and gondolas. The system is modern and efficient, with the Eiger Express significantly reducing access time to the upper slopes.
linkedResortsDescription: Grindelwald is linked to Wengen via the Kleine Scheidegg pass, allowing skiing between the two resorts. The Mürren-Schilthorn area is on the opposite side of the valley and is not connected by lifts, but the Jungfrau Ski Region pass covers all three resorts. The combined area offers 206 km of pistes and 44 lifts. The Eiger Express provides fast access to the upper slopes from Grindelwald.

terrainDescription: The ski area spans from 847m to 2027m, with 52 km of pistes (41% beginner, 53% intermediate, 6% advanced). The terrain is dominated by wide, well-groomed blue and red runs, such as the Familienabfahrt (a gentle blue ideal for families) and the FIS Wengeralm (a competition-grade red). The Fulseck descent offers a 10 km run from summit to valley with views of the Grossglockner and Dachstein mountains. The Kreuzkogel area provides short but steep red runs, and the only black runs are the RSL Hochbrand and a few short expert pitches. The slopes are predominantly south-east facing, with 90% covered by snowmaking.
offPisteDescription: Marked off-piste routes exist on the backside of Fulseck and around Seekar, offering powder skiing when conditions allow. The Hüttschlag Valley provides less crowded backcountry terrain. However, some off-piste itineraries have been reported as closed due to avalanche risk. The ungroomed red runs off the Fulseck-Kieserl ridge provide patrolled but unpisted lines. Freeriders can also explore the Kreuzkogel and Fulseck Ridge with a local guide. The resort has a snowpark with kickers and boxes for freestyle.
valueDescription: A day ski pass for the 2025/26 season costs €78.50 for adults, €59 for youth, and €39.50 for children. The resort offers several promotions: free ski passes for children up to 15 during Sun Skiing and Easter Family Special, a Young Family Ticket allowing parents to take turns skiing, Junior Weekend Discounts, and a Best Ager program with free skiing for those over 60. The Ski amadé Special in January provides discounted passes. Many mountain huts offer low-cost children's lunches. Self-catering apartments are available near the slopes.
familyDescription: The resort is very family-friendly with dedicated children's areas in both Dorfgastein and Großarl. In Dorfgastein, the Gasti Snow Park at the valley station features conveyor belts and a platter lift, plus an indoor ski kindergarten for children from age 3. The Angerer ski school has a children's area with a magic carpet. In Großarl, the Lackner ski school offers an adventure world with an indoor play area and lunchtime supervision. The Adventure Meadow Fischbacher is a free practice area with a magic carpet and platter lift. The Funslope at Kreuzhöhe includes tunnels, waves, and banked turns. The Snow Tunnel on the Kristall family slope adds a playful element. Ski schools offer multilingual instruction for children from age 4.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski centers on mountain huts and a few base-area bars. Thomaselli at the bottom of Dorfgastein is a popular spot for drinks on the deck. The Grossarltal side has several huts serving food and drinks. Evening events include torch-lit ski descents, night tobogganing, and mountain concerts. The resort also hosts a gondola dinner experience. The scene is relaxed and family-oriented rather than rowdy.
resortCharacterDescription: Grossarl is a traditional alpine village with historic buildings, known as the 'Valley of Mountain Huts' due to its many rustic mountain huts. Dorfgastein is a smaller village at the entrance to the Gastein valley. The area is surrounded by the Hohe Tauern National Park, offering scenic alpine scenery. The resort has a genuine, authentic atmosphere with local farmers and hoteliers preserving centuries-old customs. Accommodation includes traditional hotels, guesthouses, and farm stays.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 17 lifts: 5 gondolas, 8 chairlifts, and 4 surface lifts. A new 10-seater gondola serves the Großarl side. The lifts are modern with short waiting times and comfortable boarding. The network includes high-speed chairlifts and weather-protected gondolas. The ski area is connected by a flat ridge run between Kreuzkogel and Fulseck. Free ski buses link the villages to the lifts.
linkedResortsDescription: The resort is part of the Ski amadé network, which includes 760 km of pistes across five regions: Grossarltal, Gasteinertal, Salzburger Sportwelt, Hochkönig, and Schladming-Dachstein. A single ski pass grants access to all these areas. Free ski buses connect to neighboring resorts such as Alpendorf in St. Johann im Pongau and the Gastein valley (Bad Hofgastein, Bad Gastein). The resort itself is a linked ski circuit between Grossarl and Dorfgastein.

terrainDescription: The ski area is set in wooded terrain with gentle, spacious slopes descending towards the valley. It offers 31 km of pistes ranging from blue to black, including a permanent race track, a boardercross, and a fun park. The longest run is the Vacca-Talabfahrt at 7.5 km with a vertical drop of 1,200 m, and the most difficult run is the FIS-Hang at 2 km with a 450 m vertical drop. The slopes are snow-sure from December to early April.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are CHF61 for an adult day ticket, CHF46 for youth, and CHF30 for children. The resort is small and quiet, potentially offering a more affordable alternative to larger Swiss resorts, but no further details on accommodation or package deals are provided.
familyDescription: The resort is family-oriented with a dedicated Kinderland featuring magic carpets, a ghost train, a ski carousel, and snowmobile fun for children. There is a toboggan run accessible by chairlift. Childcare is available for 7 hours a day at CHF99 without lunch. The ski school has 15 instructors and offers lessons for children. Free shuttle buses connect the village to the valley station.
resortCharacterDescription: Grüsch is a traditional Swiss village with a harmonious, quiet atmosphere. The valley station is just outside the village, connected by free shuttle buses. The resort offers a 360-degree panorama from the high plateau, with activities including skiing, snowboarding, sledging, cross-country skiing, and winter hiking.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 10 lifts including 2 gondolas, 1 quad chairlift, and 7 surface lifts, with a total capacity of 8,000 persons per hour. The main gondola runs from the valley station to the Schwänzelegg area. No information on age or modern features is available.

terrainDescription: The longest run is 10 km, descending from Le Videmanette (2,151 m) to Rougemont (992 m).
resortCharacterDescription: Rougemont is a traditional Swiss village with a chic and beautiful character, offering a quieter alternative to Gstaad proper. The resort features a gondola from Rougemont to Le Videmanette, and the village has a train station on the MOB line.
linkedResortsDescription: Gstaad - Saanen - Rougemont is part of the larger Gstaad Mountain Rides ski area, which includes over 200 km of pistes across six sectors, including Eggli, Wispile, Rinderberg, Schönried, and Glacier 3000. Although lifts do not directly link the two main ski areas east and west of Gstaad, the free ski bus, Post Bus, and MOB railway line provide easy connections between them.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Götschenkopf is small, with 6 runs totaling 8.8 km of skiable terrain. The longest run is 2.5 km. The terrain includes a FIS downhill run and a black run, and the slopes are generally steep, making it unsuitable for beginners. The runs are described as beautiful and long for the size of the area.
valueDescription: Lift passes are priced at €30 for a day pass for adults and €128 for a six-day pass. Visitors have noted that the resort is relatively expensive for its size.
familyDescription: The steep terrain and lack of beginner-friendly slopes make Götschenkopf less suitable for families with novice skiers. The resort is better suited to intermediate and advanced skiers.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the Berggasthof Götschen and several bars in the nearby village of Bischofswiesen, offering a relaxed atmosphere for food and drinks.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one triple chairlift and three surface lifts, operating from 9:00 to 16:00 daily. The lifts are relatively modern but the area is small.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Hauser Kaibling is dominated by wide, groomed runs suitable for beginners and intermediates, with a dedicated 24,000 m² practice area (Wollis Kids Park) at the valley station and a 1,460 m XXL Funslope. The slopes are predominantly north-facing (61% according to skiweather.eu), which helps preserve snow quality. The highest point at 2,015 m offers panoramic views of the Dachstein massif. Two valley runs lead directly to the 8-seater gondola, and the mountain features 15 ski huts with table service.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for Hauser Kaibling costs €78.50 (adult) as of 2026, with a 6-day pass at €414. Children born in 2020 or later ski for free across the 4-mountain area. Accommodation in Haus im Ennstal is generally more affordable than in Schladming town, with self-catering apartments and guesthouses available near the slopes. The resort offers an online early booking bonus, and parking is free and guaranteed at the valley station.
familyDescription: Hauser Kaibling is well-suited for families, with the 24,000 m² Wolli's Kids Park at the valley station featuring three magic carpets (two free) and a button lift. Children up to age 6 ski for free. The ski school (Ski- und Snowboardschule Haus im Ennstal) offers English-speaking instruction. The XXL Funslope provides additional fun for all ages, and the valley station has a heated ski depot with boot heaters. The village of Haus im Ennstal is compact and walkable, with ski school meeting points close to accommodation.
apresSkiDescription: The AlmArenA at the valley station is the main après-ski bar, offering drinks and live music. There are 15 mountain huts with table service and sunny terraces, such as the Krummholzhütte and d'Genussalm. For a larger après-ski scene, Schladming town (10 minutes by car) has the Hohenhaus Tenne, Europe's largest après-ski disco, and several other bars and restaurants.
resortCharacterDescription: Haus im Ennstal is a traditional Austrian village at the foot of the mountain, with a practical layout. The valley station area is modern with a gondola, ski depot, and parking. The resort is not purpose-built but has a mix of traditional and modern infrastructure. The village itself is quiet and family-oriented, with a few restaurants and shops. The mountain huts on the slopes retain a rustic alpine character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system at Hauser Kaibling includes a gondola (Kaibling 8er), a historic cable car (the 'Bondola'), seven chairlifts (including the new 10-seater Senderbahn and 8-seater Mitterhausalm I for the connection to Planai), and two surface lifts. The lifts are modern and efficient, with the gondola and chairlifts being high-speed. The cable car is the oldest and has a distinctive design. The lift network connects seamlessly to the wider 4-mountain area.
linkedResortsDescription: Hauser Kaibling is one of four mountains in the Schladming 4-Mountain Ski Circuit (4-Berge-Schaukel), along with Planai, Hochwurzen, and Reiteralm. The four mountains offer a combined 123 km of pistes and 44 lifts, connected by a new 10-seater gondola and 8-seater chairlift from winter 2025/26. The area is also part of the larger Ski amadé network, which includes 760 km of slopes across multiple regions. The link allows skiers to tour between mountains without taking off skis, with Hauser Kaibling serving as the eastern gateway.

terrainDescription: The ski area sits between 1,130m and 1,559m, with slopes predominantly facing north (68%) and west (31%). The terrain is mostly gentle to moderate: 10 km of novice runs, 20 km of intermediate runs, and 2 km of expert runs. The longest run is the Waldschneise at 2.5 km with a 400m vertical drop. The most difficult run is the 'Iseler Standard' (black), also 2.5 km with 400m elevation change. A freeride area near the Iselerbahn offers off-piste opportunities. Many runs are tree-lined, providing shelter from wind.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are moderate: a day pass for adults costs €52, a six-day pass €264, and a child day pass €24. Accommodation and dining costs are typical for a German resort, with self-catering apartments and traditional Bavarian restaurants available. The resort is considered good value for families and beginners compared to larger Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: The resort is family-friendly with a dedicated Snow Kids' Land for children, featuring a ski school with English-speaking instructors. Nursery slopes are gentle and separate from main traffic. Off-slope activities include a 3.5 km toboggan run, 60 km of winter hiking trails, and night skiing every Thursday from 17:30 to 21:00 on floodlit pistes. The village is compact and walkable, making it easy for families to get around.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and family-oriented, with cosy mountain huts and taverns serving Bavarian specialties. Popular spots include the Alpenhotel Oberjoch, Berggasthof Kammeregg, and Hindelang Alm for drinks. Night skiing on Thursdays provides an additional evening activity. The resort also has a MINI Snowpark with a bag jump and chill area.
resortCharacterDescription: Oberjoch is a small, traditional Bavarian village in the Allgäu Alps, with a population of around 200. It is one of the highest ski villages in Germany, at an elevation of 1,140m. The village has a compact, walkable layout with alpine charm, featuring traditional architecture and a relaxed atmosphere. It is not a purpose-built resort but a historic settlement with a long skiing tradition dating back to 1904.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 5 lifts: 4 chairlifts and 1 tow lift, with a total capacity of approximately 12,000 persons per hour. The main access is via the Iselerbahn chairlift. Lifts are modern and well-maintained. Night skiing is available on Thursday evenings on 3 km of floodlit pistes, served by the Iselerbahn and Idealhanglift.

terrainDescription: The ski area on the Höss mountain features three valley runs that descend through long forest corridors to Hinterstoder. The Hannes Trinkl World Cup run, a 3.5 km black slope, is the only World Cup course in Upper Austria. The Inferno piste has a maximum gradient of 70 percent. Panoramic runs from the 2,000 m Schafkogel peak offer views of the Totes Gebirge range. The Hutterer Böden plateau provides wide, gentle runs for beginners and families, while the Hirschkogelbahn area includes a carving slope and family runs.
offPisteDescription: Freeriders can access the Loigistal area for off-piste skiing. The resort also offers marked freeride routes and untouched powder fields. Additionally, there are two signposted snowshoe trails for exploring the backcountry.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are dynamic, with a day pass for adults starting at €44 and a 6-day pass at €219. Children's day passes are €23, and a 6-day pass is €114. Families can benefit from the KidsCard mini, which costs €5 per day for children born up to 2020 when a parent purchases a ski pass. Parking at the valley station is free, and a free ski bus operates throughout the Pyhrn-Priel region, including from the train station to the slopes.
familyDescription: The Sunny Kids Park on the Hutterer Böden plateau features magic carpet lifts, ski carousels, conveyor belts, and wave tracks, designed for children learning to ski. The ski school offers lessons for children, and there are dedicated family runs on the Höss. The resort also has a family slope and a children's area with practice lifts. The valley runs are accessible for beginners, and the free ski bus makes it easy to get around.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski begins on the slopes at the Hutterer Böden, where skiers can enjoy drinks in the sun. The Lögerhütt'n bar is known for its lively atmosphere, and the SIGLU-Bar near the valley station car park is popular after the lifts close. For late-night entertainment, the Party Stadl offers music and dancing. There are six bars and pubs in the resort.
resortCharacterDescription: Hinterstoder is a traditional alpine village at 600 m elevation, surrounded by the limestone peaks of the Totes Gebirge range, including the Großer Priel (2,514 m) and Spitzmauer (2,446 m). The village has a main street with a few restaurants, cafés, and a Spar supermarket. The Schiederweiher, a small artificial lake, is a local landmark. The resort has developed from a small alpine settlement into a World Cup venue, but retains a quiet, family-friendly character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes a modern 10-person gondola (Hössbahn) from the valley to the Hutterer Böden, a high-speed detachable chairlift (Höss-Express) to the Höss summit, and the Hirschkogelbahn, Upper Austria's first 10-person gondola. The network comprises 14 lifts in total, including gondolas, chairlifts, and surface lifts, with a combined capacity of 25,500 persons per hour. Lifts operate from 08:30 to 16:30.
linkedResortsDescription: Hinterstoder is part of the Pyhrn-Priel holiday region. A combined lift pass is available with the nearby Wurzeralm ski area, which is accessible via a free ski bus. Other nearby resorts include Kasberg and Tauplitzalm.

terrainDescription: The combined Hirmentaz and Les Habères ski areas offer 43 marked pistes (8 green, 16 blue, 17 red, 2 black) covering approximately 40–50 km of slopes. The terrain is spread across two linked family-friendly sites, with runs like La Combe and Les Chavannes providing variety. The slopes offer views of Lake Geneva and Mont Blanc.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are relatively low compared to major Alpine resorts, with an adult day pass at €35 and a six-day pass ranging from €158 to €183. A 'Ski malin' Wednesday offer reduces the day pass to €23 during low season. Accommodation and dining are also noted as affordable, with prices half to a third of those in more famous resorts like Chamonix or Verbier.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as family-friendly, with a dedicated children's ski play area called 'Le Monde de Perce-Neige' at Les Habères. A day nursery is available, and the two linked sites offer gentle slopes suitable for beginners. The quiet, uncrowded atmosphere is noted as a plus for families.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key, with venues like Le Refuge and La Grange offering local cuisine and a cozy atmosphere. The resort has chalet restaurants serving Savoyard specialities, and nearby spas provide relaxation. Nightlife is limited; there are no large discos, making it suitable for those seeking a quiet evening.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort consists of two linked family-friendly sites, Hirmentaz in Bellevaux and Les Habères in Habère-Poche, both set in traditional mountain villages. The area retains a village atmosphere with authentic Savoyard architecture, rather than being a large purpose-built complex. The Hirmentaz station was developed from 1963 by local artisans, adding lifts gradually.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises around 17–23 lifts, including chairlifts and surface lifts, with a total uphill capacity of about 16,000 skiers per hour. The network has been developed incrementally since 1963, with older lifts replaced over time, such as a 1977 chairlift replaced in 2008. Snowmaking covers 15 km of runs with 10 cannons.
linkedResortsDescription: The Hirmentaz and Les Habères ski areas are linked, forming a combined domain of approximately 50 km of pistes. The two stations are located on the slopes of the same mountain, Hirmentaz, in the communes of Bellevaux and Habère-Poche. A single lift pass grants access to both areas.

terrainDescription: The Hoch-Imst ski area offers 9 km of groomed pistes between 1,000 m and 2,100 m, split into 2 km easy, 5 km medium, and 2 km difficult. The steepest run, Pratz Bäume, has a gradient of up to 60% and is suitable only for expert skiers. A large practice slope with a platter lift sits next to the valley station, and a fun park with 15 obstacles (public, beginner, and pro lines) is located at the Malchbach lift. The long run from the Alpjoch top station is a popular intermediate descent.
valueDescription: A 1-day adult lift pass costs €47 in high season (22 Dec–10 Jan, 1 Feb–28 Feb) and €40 in low season. Children's passes are €24.40 and €20.80 respectively. A family pass (parents plus own children born 2007–2019) costs €118.40 for one day in high season. Free children's ski courses are offered during certain weeks. The Imst Card provides free use of public buses in the Outdoorregion Imst.
familyDescription: The ski area is marketed as family-friendly with a gentle topography and observable terrain. A spacious practice slope with a platter lift is located right at the valley station, separate from faster traffic. Two natural toboggan runs (Untermarkter Alm 4 km, Obermarkter Alm 6 km) and the Alpine Coaster (3.5 km) provide off-piste family activities. Free children's ski courses are offered during selected weeks. The two 10-seater gondolas are barrier-free and can accommodate prams and toboggans.
