Terrain is split 59% intermediate, 28% advanced, and 13% expert. The forest setting provides shelter from wind, and the slopes are groomed for carving, with some tree-lined sections allowing adventurous skiing after snowfall.
valueDescription: A day lift ticket costs €49 (approximately 1,200 CZK). On-slope beer is under €4. Free parking is available with over 2,000 spaces across multiple lots. Accommodation in the nearby UNESCO-listed spa town of Karlovy Vary (15 km away) is available for under €100 per night in high-quality hotels. Ski-in/ski-out lodging is limited and often fully booked. The resort offers online ski pass purchases at a discount, and multi-day passes include access to the German side via the InterSkiregion without extra cost.
familyDescription: The resort has dedicated children's areas including a baby ski park and five children's conveyor belts. A ski school offers lessons for beginners and advanced skiers and snowboarders of all ages, with English-speaking instructors available. There is a tubing lane and two terrain parks. Free parking and ski buses reduce logistics. However, visitor reviews note that on peak days (e.g., New Year's Eve) queues can exceed one hour, and the single cable car serving multiple slopes creates bottlenecks, making it less ideal for families with young children on busy weekends.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort has two main entrance points: Jáchymov (a historic radon spa town) and Loučná pod Klínovcem, both with free parking. The lift infrastructure mixes modern high-speed quad chairlifts (e.g., Prima Express, Suzuki) with older T-bar surface lifts, particularly in the Neklid section. The slopes are entirely within fir forest, not above treeline, offering protected skiing. The base area is purpose-built with ticket offices, ski rental, and a few restaurants, but lacks a traditional alpine village atmosphere. The nearby town of Karlovy Vary provides historic spa architecture and accommodation.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 18 lifts: 3 high-speed quad chairlifts, 1 fixed-grip quad chair, 1 triple chair, and 13 surface lifts (T-bars and platters). There are also 5 children's conveyor belts. The main access from the base uses the Prima Express quad and the Suzuki quad. A free ski bus runs every 10 minutes between Klínovec and the German resort Fichtelberg, taking 15 minutes. Visitor reviews report that queues can be very long on weekends and holidays, with waits of up to 1.5 hours at popular lifts, and the single cable car (Suzuki) is often overcrowded.
linkedResortsDescription: Klínovec is linked with the German resort Fichtelberg via the InterSkiregion project. A multi-day ski pass (minimum 1.5 days) purchased at either resort is valid across both areas, giving access to 47 km of slopes in total. A free ski bus runs every 10 minutes between the resorts, taking 15 minutes. This allows skiers to cross the border and ski in two countries on the same ticket. The combined area includes 25 lifts and a variety of terrain on both sides of the mountain.

terrainDescription: The ski area features wide, clear slopes that are fully artificially snowed and lit. The longest run is 3.2 km. The terrain is predominantly gentle, with a mix of beginner, intermediate, and advanced runs. The slopes are well-maintained and offer good visibility.
valueDescription: The resort offers free parking at both base stations (Pstruží and near Abertamy). Lift passes are affordable compared to Alpine resorts, making it a budget-friendly option. Ski equipment rental and ski school are available on-site.
familyDescription: The resort has one of the largest children's ski parks in the Czech Republic, equipped with four moving carpet lifts (83m, 42m, 40m, 30m), a children's ski carousel, and snow-tubing. A ski school is available, and there are sledding tracks for families. The children's centre is located near the slopes.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski options include the Apres Ski bar U Dostyho on the slope, the Skibar on another slope, and the mountain-top restaurant Plešivec with panoramic views. Additional dining is available at the bistro Stodola and restaurant Fabrika Art in the Zelené Údolí chalet.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a modern, purpose-built ski area in the Ore Mountains, with a summit at 1,028 m featuring a lookout tower, hotel, and restaurant. The base stations are at Pstruží and near Abertamy, with free parking. It is one of the newest ski resorts in the Czech Republic, using modern technology for snowmaking and lifts.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of three four-seater cable cars (including two express detachable chairs) and six surface lifts, for a total of nine lifts. The cable cars have a high transport capacity of up to 2,400 people per hour each, minimizing queues. The slopes are fully artificially snowed and lit.

terrainDescription: The ski area stretches between Bertoldi and Passo Vezzena across the Alpe Cimbra plateau, with slopes winding through alpine coniferous forests and clearings. All parts are connected by easy runs, allowing beginners and children to explore the entire area. Specific named runs include the blue runs 'Le Stock', 'Vezzena', 'Torrion', 'Le Cogole', and 'Fratelle', the red 'Ust' run leading to Malga Laghetto, and the black 'Avez del Prinzep' run descending from Monte Cucco. A four-seater chairlift serves the summit of Monte Cucco, with descents to Passo Vezzena and Malga Rivetta. A snowpark is also available for freestyle enthusiasts.
valueDescription: A day lift pass for the main season costs €48 for adults, €36 for youth, and €26 for children when purchased at the resort. Online prices are slightly higher: €49 weekday / €55 weekend for adults, with similar proportional pricing for youth and children. A six-day pass is available for around €250. The Alpe Cimbra full-area pass, covering Lavarone, Folgaria, and Fiorentini, offers access to 104 km of slopes and is priced higher than the local Lavarone-only pass. Accommodation and dining in the area are noted for being reasonably priced compared to larger Alpine resorts.
familyDescription: Lavarone is widely considered one of the most beginner-friendly ski areas in Trentino, with four dedicated beginner areas equipped with magic carpets at Lavarone Bertoldi and Passo Vezzena. The Lavarone Ski School employs over 50 instructors offering lessons in skiing and snowboarding. All slopes in the ski area are connected by easy runs, so families can ski together across the entire domain without encountering difficult terrain. The atmosphere is calm and uncrowded, with well-groomed pistes that are safe for children.
resortCharacterDescription: Lavarone is a small, peaceful ski resort set on the forested Alpe Cimbra plateau in Trentino. The landscape is characterised by alpine coniferous forests, clearings, and cozy wooden mountain huts (malghe) that serve as rest stops. The resort has a quiet, traditional atmosphere rather than a purpose-built feel, with the village of Lavarone itself offering a relaxed base. The ski area is compact and uncrowded, surrounded by gentle hills and scenic woodland.
liftSystemDescription: The ski area is served by 14 lifts: 6 chairlifts (including a four-seater chairlift to Monte Cucco), 2 T-bars, and 6 magic carpets. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with the entire area covered by automatic snowmaking. The lift network is compact and easy to navigate, with all parts of the ski area connected by easy slopes. A wider Alpe Cimbra pass also provides access to lifts in Folgaria and Fiorentini.
linkedResortsDescription: Lavarone is part of the Alpe Cimbra ski area, which also includes the Folgaria and Fiorentini ski zones. These are connected via the 'Skitour dei Forti' ski carousel, allowing skiers to explore a combined total of 104 km of slopes with a single Alpe Cimbra pass. The linked resorts offer additional terrain across the plateau, with bus connections between the sectors.

terrainDescription: The ski area covers 72km of pistes across 52 runs, with 56% graded red and 8% black, dropping through open alpine meadows and dense forest beneath Monte Civetta's 3,220m rock face; the terrain is linked village-to-village from Alleghe through Selva di Cadore to Val di Zoldo, with the north-facing Val di Zoldo sector holding cold snow and firm conditions into the afternoon after storms, while the Alleghe-facing slopes soften earlier; Gran Zuita descends 1,100m from Col Fioret (2,082m) to Alleghe Lake (1,000m) across three linked slopes with 360-degree Dolomite panoramas including views of the Sella Group, Marmolada, and Croda da Lago.
offPisteDescription: Forest terrain between marked runs holds snow well after storms and provides accessible tree skiing, with the north-facing Pelmo flanks offering powder stashes; above the treeline, steeper bowls and gullies reward exploration when conditions are right, while Monte Civetta's flanks present challenging touring routes for experienced mountaineers; the Marmolada glacier and surrounding Sella and Tofana groups provide high-alpine terrain accessible on the Dolomiti Superski pass, with the Marmolada summit descent being one of the most celebrated in the range; freeriding is prohibited in the resort itself and a guide is recommended for all off-piste exploration.
valueDescription: Day lift passes cost €67 for adults (€62-67 depending on pre-purchase), with children paying €22-47 and seniors €47-61; food and drink prices are described as very reasonable, with Rifugio Palafavera noted for plentiful, cheap, and delicious cuisine; accommodation is mostly family-run hotels and holiday flats that are budget-friendly; night skiing from the Pecol gondola provides additional value; however, parking fees apply in Alleghe.
familyDescription: Nursery slopes with surface lifts exist at each base village, with Palafavera's being the gentlest and most sheltered, though there are no dedicated green-graded runs; the ski area is described as ideal for families with gentle progression terrain for returning beginners, quiet slopes with less pressure from faster skiers, and a family-friendly snowpark at Col dei Baldi (1,470m) with kickers, rails, and boxes for children and beginners; Alleghe has a population of around 1,100, offering a minimarket, several hotels, and an ice rink, with ski buses connecting to other areas twice hourly.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is described as quite relaxed with a rating of 3 out of 5 stars; the only true umbrella bar is Fontanabona, located directly on the Coldai slope at 1,497m, requiring departure before 4:30pm to catch the last lift to the valley; additional bars include Bar at Crep di Pecol and Baita Ru on the valley run to Pecol, with cozy atmosphere at valley stations to end the ski day; evening options are limited with no party bars, though La Botte and Bar Pizzeria Al Sole offer local cuisine and nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Alleghe is a traditional mountain village of around 1,100 residents sitting on the shore of Lago di Alleghe, an alpine lake created by a landslide in 1771, with Monte Civetta's thousand-metre rock face reflected in the water; Selva di Cadore is a collective of Ladin-speaking villages in the Val Fiorentina preserving the ancient Dolomite language and Veneto culinary traditions; Val di Zoldo remains less developed for tourism than other UNESCO Dolomites areas, offering authentic historically-rich villages; the overall feel is described as a place tourism hasn't yet reshaped, with the quieter and more authentic side of the Dolomites compared to the more developed South Tyrolean resorts to the north.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 23 lifts including 3 gondolas and 10 chairlifts with 10 surface drags, with six high-speed quads on the main sectors; lift capacity is 34,010 people per hour with opening hours 08:30-16:30; the Alleghe-Civetta gondola and Zoldo Valley lifts service the main verticals; the system links Alleghe, Selva di Cadore, Palafavera, and Val di Zoldo, enabling village-to-village circuit skiing; 98% of slopes are covered by a modern snowmaking system with 285 snow cannons and 14 snowcats maintaining conditions.
linkedResortsDescription: Civetta is part of the Dolomiti Superski network, one of 12 ski areas comprising 1,200km of pistes across the Dolomites; the ski area itself links Alleghe, Selva di Cadore, Palafavera, and Val di Zoldo with ski bus connections to Marmolada glacier, Cortina d'Ampezzo, and the famous Sellaronda ski circuit (accessible by car in about an hour); the area is a major link in the Grande Guerra (Great War) ski tour, a First World War history route; day trips to Arabba, Alta Badia, and Val Gardena are feasible on the Dolomiti Superski pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 46 km of slopes, with 54% beginner (wide blue runs), 32% intermediate (red runs winding through forests and open alpine areas), and 14% advanced (including the Grubhörndl black run and Hochwena-Abfahrt). The Family Express chairlift serves the main learning zone, and the Lofi Funline provides a small snow park with waves, jumps, and banked turns. The longest run is 9 km. Snowmaking covers 36 km of slopes, and 90 snow machines with a 135,000 m³ water reservoir support snow reliability.
valueDescription: Day tickets cost €62 for adults, €49.50 for youth, and €31 for children. Family deals include a one-day ticket for 2 adults and 1 child at €143.50 in high season, and a 6-day family pass at €737.50. Children up to 18 ski free on Saturdays when accompanied by two paying parents. A season ticket valid across the 3 Länder Freizeit-Arena (covering Lofer, Steinplatte/Waidring, Winkelmoos, St. Johann in Tirol, Pillersee/Buchensteinwand, Unken/Heutal, Kirchdorf, and Erpfendorf) is available. Self-catering apartments and family-run hotels keep accommodation costs moderate.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a family paradise. The children's area features a 140-meter-long covered conveyor belt for all-weather comfort. The Family Express chairlift serves gentle blue runs ideal for learning. Two child-safe 8-seater chairlifts are in operation. Ski schools offer English-speaking instructors. The village is compact and walkable, with a ski bus to the slopes. The main road is in a tunnel, keeping the village quiet. Accommodation includes family-run hotels and apartments.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and family-oriented, with hotel bars and local pubs such as the Dankl. Mountain huts like the Kechtalm serve traditional Austrian dishes. There is no chaotic nightlife; the scene is quiet and authentic.
resortCharacterDescription: Lofer is a traditional market town in the Saalach valley, with a historic parish church and typical Tyrolean architecture. The village is compact and quiet, as the main road bypasses it through a tunnel. Accommodation is in family-run hotels and apartments. The resort is not purpose-built; it has a working village character.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 10 lifts: 2 gondolas (Almbahn I and II), 2 eight-person chairlifts (including the SenderExpress and Family Express), 1 six-person chairlift, and 5 surface lifts. Three chairlifts have weather shields and heated seats. The gondolas are modern and efficient. The system is well-maintained and queues are rare outside peak periods.
linkedResortsDescription: The ski area is linked with the Heutal lifts in Unken, forming the 'Almenwelt Lofer & Heutal' region. A season ticket (3 Länder Freizeit-Arena) also covers Steinplatte/Waidring, Winkelmoos, St. Johann in Tirol, Pillersee/Buchensteinwand, Kirchdorf, and Erpfendorf. However, the main ski area is a small family resort with a connection to Unken.

terrainDescription: The ski area is situated between 896 m and 1,300 m, with slopes almost entirely covered by artificial snow. The terrain is dominated by easy blue runs, making up 64% of the 12.5 km of pistes, with intermediate reds at 20% and difficult blacks at 16%. The Hennesteck summit offers panoramic views and a mountain hut. For variety, the Anna-Park provides obstacles, kickers, a wave track, and a boardercross course, while the Anna-Land is a dedicated beginner and children's area with a fairytale forest. The Fis Abfahrt 1a is a noted black run for advanced skiers.
valueDescription: The NÖ Bergerlebnispass season pass covers 10 ski areas in Lower Austria, including Annaberg, with a super pre-sale price of €410 for adults and free for children when purchasing two adult passes. A single lift ticket also works across three linked ski mountains on the Mariazellerbahn line: Annaberg, Gemeindealpe Mitterbach, and Bürgeralpe, providing access to 50 km of runs. A free ski bus connects Bürgeralpe and Gemeindealpe. The resort is noted for being affordable, with a 6-day adult pass in the Dachstein West region (if applicable) around €170, though this may vary.
familyDescription: The resort is particularly popular with families. The Anna-Land children's area features a fairytale forest with speaking animals, a children's playground, and specially equipped beginner slopes. The largest ski school in Lower Austria offers lessons for children. Off-slope activities include tobogganing (floodlit on Saturdays), snowshoeing, winter hiking, cross-country skiing, and horse-drawn sleigh rides. The village is traffic-free in the centre, and the ski area is well-organised with short distances.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, with two bars and pubs in the village and three restaurants. On the mountain, the Anna-Alm on the Hennesteck and the Eisbar offer refreshments, while valley options include the Teichstüberl and Reidlhütte. The scene is relaxed, with rustic huts and a cosy atmosphere rather than a rowdy nightlife.
resortCharacterDescription: Annaberg is a traditional alpine village in the Mostviertel region, with charming accommodations and rustic ski huts typical of the area. The resort offers views of the Ötscher mountain. It is not a purpose-built station but a genuine village with a cozy, family-friendly atmosphere. The village centre is walkable, and the ski area is directly accessible from the village.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts, including 2 chairlifts, 2 drag lifts, and 2 T-bars, with a total capacity of 5,652 persons per hour. The lifts have been modernised in recent years and are described as having the latest technology. The main lift takes guests up to the Hennesteck summit at 1,300 m. Lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:00 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: Annaberg is part of the Schiland Voralpen association, which links three ski mountains along the Mariazellerbahn rail line: Annaberg, Gemeindealpe Mitterbach, and Bürgeralpe. A single lift ticket is valid in all three areas, and a free ski bus runs between Bürgeralpe and Gemeindealpe. Additionally, the NÖ Bergerlebnispass season pass covers 10 ski areas in Lower Austria, including Annaberg, offering access to 51 lifts and 125 km of slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski area comprises 12 groomed runs totaling 17 km, with 6 km novice, 7 km intermediate, and 4 km expert terrain. Slopes are predominantly north-facing (65% of terrain), with a black run descending from the Hahnenwipfel peak offering a long north-facing descent. The resort's summit is at 1,600 m and base at 680 m, giving a vertical drop of 920 m. A fun park is available for freestylers.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste opportunities are primarily ski touring and snowshoeing. Ski tourers can ascend 800 vertical meters from the valley to the summit, and free ski touring nights are held every Thursday from 5 p.m. on the valley run. Snowshoeing and winter hiking routes are available from the mountain station, with a new family adventure trail planned. No specific off-piste skiing quality is described.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €49 for adults, €36.50 for youths, and €24.50 for children (2025/26 season). Free parking is available directly in front of the mountain railways. The resort is smaller and less crowded than larger Carinthian destinations, offering a budget-friendly option for families and beginners.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as a family adventure mountain with a dedicated ski school (HAPPY Skischule Dreiländereck) offering professional courses and ski hire. An 8.4 km natural toboggan run, a panoramic ice skating rink at the mountain station, and a planned family adventure trail with interactive stations provide non-skiing activities. The slopes are predominantly easy (74% beginner) and intermediate (13%), with gentle terrain suitable for children.
apresSkiDescription: On-mountain apres-ski is limited to the Panorama Mountain Restaurant with a sun terrace serving Austrian specialties. The nearest lively apres-ski scene and nightclubs are in Villach, a 20-minute drive away. Some laid-back spots for hot drinks and meals are available in the base area and nearby villages.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is a small, traditional mountain ski area on the Dreiländereck peak, located at the tripoint of Austria, Italy, and Slovenia. The base area at Seltschach has free parking and a few facilities, but no purpose-built village. The nearby town of Arnoldstein (2 km away) is a historic market town with a ruined abbey, offering accommodation and services. The resort has been operating since the 1930s and is popular for day trips from Villach and across the borders.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 7 lifts: one 3-seater chairlift and six T-bar surface lifts. Total uphill capacity is 8,100 persons per hour. The system is small and serves the 17 km of pistes. Modern snow-making equipment covers the entire skiable terrain, ensuring consistent conditions despite the low base altitude.

terrainDescription: The ski area is served by two chairlifts (Stafelalpe and Glatthorn) reaching 2,000 m, plus four surface lifts including the Guggernülli lift. The main runs are blue and red, with three black ski routes (Hahnenkopf South, East, North) starting from the top of the Stafelalpe lift. The valley area has practice lifts and a ski kindergarten. Night skiing is offered every Thursday from 18:00 to 21:00 on two illuminated blue runs at the Guggernülli lift. The terrain is predominantly gentle, suitable for beginners and intermediates, with a few challenging routes for advanced skiers.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €56.50 for adults (2025/26 season), with a six-day pass at €226. The resort is small and less expensive than larger nearby areas like Damüls-Mellau. Accommodation includes hotels and apartments directly on the slopes, with 7 hotels and 415 tourist beds. A 3-valley super ski pass is available, covering Faschina, Damüls-Mellau, and Lechtal, offering access to a much larger ski area for a combined price.
familyDescription: The resort is family-oriented with a ski kindergarten and a miniclub for children from age three. The Ski and Snowboard School Faschina-Raggal offers lessons for children and beginners. Practice lifts, a magic carpet, and a children's carousel are located in the valley area. The ski school provides certified instructors who take time with children. The quiet atmosphere and traffic-free village make it suitable for families.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is quiet, with a few mountain huts such as Franz-Josef-Hütte and Café Lari Fari offering drinks and local cuisine. For livelier nightlife, visitors take the ski bus to Damüls, which has several bars and pubs. The resort has 5 bars and pubs in total, but the scene is subdued and focused on relaxation.
resortCharacterDescription: Fontanella is a picturesque small village in the Grosses Walsertal, with traditional Walser architecture. The ski lifts start directly in the hamlet of Faschina at 1,500 m, where hotels and restaurants are located along the slopes. The atmosphere is quiet and family-friendly, with a compact layout that allows easy access to lifts and accommodation.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 6 lifts: two chairlifts (Stafelalpe and Glatthorn) and four surface lifts, including the Guggernülli lift which operates for night skiing on Thursdays. The lifts have a combined capacity of 4,700 persons per hour. Lifts operate from 09:00 to 16:00 daily, with night skiing from 18:00 to 21:00 on Thursdays during the season. The lifts are located directly in the village, providing ski-in/ski-out access for many accommodations.
linkedResortsDescription: Faschina is part of the larger Damüls-Mellau-Faschina ski area, which offers 109 km of pistes and 27 lifts. Although there is no direct lift connection, a regular ski bus runs between Faschina and Damüls. A 3-valley super ski pass is available, covering the Great Walser Valley, Bregenzerwald, and Lechtal, providing access to a combined ski area of over 150 km of slopes.

terrainDescription: The ski area combines three mountains: Walmendingerhorn, Ifen, and Heuberg. Walmendingerhorn offers 15 km of groomed pistes suited to pleasure skiers, with a glass panoramic lift and a viewing platform. Heuberg is known as the family mountain, with over 20 km of easy, wide slopes ideal for beginners and children. Ifen provides over 25 km of varied terrain, including mostly easy pistes for families and beginners, plus challenging black runs such as the Olympiahang (1 km, 320 m vertical drop) and the Kellerloch. Three valley runs are available at Ifen. The Olympiahang is a black run used for the Olympic slope. The terrain is diverse, with 59% beginner, 30% intermediate, and 11% advanced runs according to the resort's own classification.
offPisteDescription: The Walmendingerhorn is a favourite among locals for off-piste skiing, with deep snow conditions when storms approach from the north. The Ifen area also receives significant snowfall, often recording one of the deepest alpine snowpacks in the Alps. The Gottesacker plateau on Ifen features snow dunes that are popular for winter hiking and snowshoeing, but also offer off-piste opportunities. The region's geographic location on the northern edge of the Alps brings heavy snowfall, making it a reliable destination for powder skiing.
valueDescription: A one-day adult lift pass costs €68.70, with youth passes at €52.90 and child passes at €29. The combined ski area lift pass for the Oberstdorf-Kleinwalsertal region is reasonably priced, and the Indy Pass is also valid here. The MyMountainClub Card offers discounts on lift tickets and at partner restaurants, with points that can be redeemed for further savings. Parking at the valley stations is free at Heuberg and costs €5 per day at some locations. The resort is accessible toll-free from Germany via the A7 and B19 motorways.
familyDescription: Heuberg Arena is specifically designed for families, with the Burmi Kinderland featuring mascots and child-friendly features, a ski kindergarten, a children's carpet lift, and a children's restaurant. The Heuberg slopes are wide and easy, with over 20 km of beginner terrain, and floodlit skiing is available at the Hubertuslift on certain evenings. The Ifen area also has predominantly easy pistes suitable for families. The ski school offers lessons for children, and the three villages (Mittelberg, Riezlern, Hirschegg) are connected by a free ski bus, making it easy to access the different areas. The resort is car-free in the sense that the villages are walkable and the ski bus is comprehensive.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is quiet and cosy, with small bars and huts offering drinks under the Hoher Ifen. There are three bars and pubs in the area. For livelier nightlife, visitors need to travel to larger towns in the surroundings. The mountain restaurants, such as Tafel&Zunder at Ifen and Gipfelstuba at Walmendingerhorn, offer evening dining with regional cuisine, but the focus is on relaxed end-of-day drinks rather than partying.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is set in the Kleinwalsertal valley, a traditional Austrian region accessible only from Germany. The villages of Mittelberg (1,250 m), Riezlern, and Hirschegg have alpine charm with wooden chalets and hotels. The ski infrastructure is modern, with the glass panoramic lift at Walmendingerhorn, the new 10-person gondolas Ifen I and Ifen II, and the 6-person Olympiabahn chairlift with weather protection and seat heating. The Heuberg area has surface lifts and a floodlit lift. The valley is not purpose-built but has grown organically, with a mix of traditional architecture and modern lift facilities.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 15 lifts (per known facts) across the three areas, though the combined region has 48 lifts. At Ifen, the modern 10-person gondolas Ifen I and Ifen II and the 6-person Olympiabahn chairlift with weather protection and seat heating provide comfortable access. Walmendingerhorn features a glass panoramic lift that takes skiers from the chairlift to the restaurant. Heuberg has surface lifts and a floodlit lift (Hubertuslift). The lifts operate from 8:30 to 16:30 daily, with last descents between 15:30 and 17:00 depending on the lift. Snowmaking is present, with new cannons installed at Ifen.
linkedResortsDescription: The Walmendingerhorn, Ifen, and Heuberg ski areas are combined under a single lift pass and connected by a free ski bus, forming the largest ski area in Kleinwalsertal with 67 km of pistes. They are part of the larger two-country ski region Oberstdorf Kleinwalsertal, which also includes the German resorts of Nebelhorn, Fellhorn, Söllereck, and Kanzelwand, offering a total of 130 km of slopes and 48 lifts. The Indy Pass is valid in the region. A single ticket grants access to all seven mountains across the border.

terrainDescription: The Ifen ski area offers predominantly easy and intermediate terrain, with 12.6 km of blue runs, 6 km of red runs, and 2 km of black runs. The Gottesacker plateau area provides gentle, wide slopes suitable for beginners and families. Advanced skiers can tackle the 3.5 km black Olympia run, which drops 750 vertical meters, or the steep Kellerloch run. Three valley runs allow long descents back to the base. Most slopes face east, catching morning sun and holding good snow conditions early in the day.
valueDescription: A 6-day adult lift pass costs €342.80 in low season and €360.80 in high season, with children's passes at €90.00 and free entry for children under 5. The Oberstdorf Kleinwalsertal ski pass covers all three areas (Ifen, Heuberg, Walmendingerhorn) and the wider 2-country region. Parking at the valley station costs €5 per day. Student season passes are available for €237. The Indy Pass is also valid in the region.
familyDescription: The Ifen ski area has a dedicated beginner zone at the Gaisbühllift, featuring a magic carpet and the Burmi children's world with a mascot. The slopes are predominantly easy, making it suitable for families. The connected Heuberg Arena offers a ski kindergarten, children's carpet lift, and children's restaurant. A free ski bus connects the areas, and the region has a relaxed, family-friendly atmosphere.
resortCharacterDescription: The Ifen ski area is set against the dramatic Hoher Ifen mountain (2,230 m) and the Gottesacker plateau, a karst landscape designated as a nature reserve. The area is not a purpose-built resort but a natural mountain setting, with the nearest villages being Hirschegg and Riezlern in the Kleinwalsertal valley. The ski lifts were first installed in the 1970s and have been modernized since.
liftSystemDescription: The Ifen ski area has 4 lifts: two 10-person gondolas (Ifen I and Ifen II) and a 6-person chairlift (Olympiabahn) with weather protection and heated seats, plus a surface lift (Gaisbühllift) for beginners. The lifts have a combined capacity of 5,600 persons per hour. The Olympiabahn opens early at 07:45 in March and April. The lifts were first installed in the 1970s and have been upgraded to modern standards.
linkedResortsDescription: The Ifen ski area is part of the Walmendingerhorn-Ifen-Heuberg arena, which offers 67 km of pistes and 18 lifts, connected by a free ski bus. A single ski pass covers all three areas. Additionally, the wider 2-country ski region Oberstdorf-Kleinwalsertal includes seven ski areas (Ifen, Heuberg, Walmendingerhorn, Fellhorn-Kanzelwand, Nebelhorn, Söllereck) with a total of 130–150 km of pistes and 48 lifts, also linked by free ski buses.

terrainDescription: The terrain consists of slopes below the tree line on the Großer Ötscher (1,893 m) and Kleiner Ötscher (1,560 m). There are 7 km of easy blue runs, 8 km of intermediate red runs, and 4 km of difficult black runs, with a total of 19 km of groomed pistes. The longest run is 4 km. The ski area includes a Funslope, a Snowpark, and two FIS race courses for slalom and giant slalom. The Große Ötscherpiste and Riffel Downhill are challenging runs for advanced skiers.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are described as fair, with a 15% discount available when purchasing online compared to at-sight prices. The resort is marketed as an affordable winter vacation destination. A 6-day lift pass for the combined Hochkar-Ötscher area is valued at up to €196 in package deals.
familyDescription: The resort features a Sunny Kids Park with two magic carpets and childcare, along with two ski schools offering lessons for beginners. The Kinderland area is dedicated to young children. The village of Lackenhof is car-free, making it safe for families. There is a family downhill run and a Cross Trail course for older children.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski options include 2 bars and pubs and 1 club. There are 4 restaurants in the resort.
resortCharacterDescription: Lackenhof is a small, car-free mountain village in the Ybbstaler Alps, offering an idyllic alpine setting. The resort is not purpose-built but a traditional village with ski slopes accessible from the village.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of chairlifts and surface lifts, including 2 quad chairlifts, 1 double chairlift, and 3 T-bar lifts, with additional rope lifts and 2 magic carpets in the Sunny Kids Park. The official resort website lists six lift installations.
linkedResortsDescription: The ski area is linked with the Hochkar ski resort through a joint lift pass (Hochkar-Ötscher). Both resorts are managed by Hochkar & Ötscher Tourismus GmbH. The combined area offers more extensive skiing options.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 34 km of well-groomed pistes, with 59% beginner, 26% intermediate, and 15% advanced runs. The terrain is dominated by wide, sunny slopes, with the longest family run stretching 9 km from the Loser Panorama slope down to the valley. The Loserfenster area features treeless slopes accessed by a four-seater chairlift. Easy runs are available on both the Sandling and Loser sides, while advanced skiers can find steeper FIS runs and off-piste terrain above the treeline.
offPisteDescription: The high plateau above the treeline offers deep powder fields, popular with freeriders. Marked ski touring routes attract local skiers for backcountry experiences. The area is not a freeride mecca but provides quiet climbs and untouched snow with proper avalanche awareness. The Loser mountain's southern face has been developed for skiing, but the surrounding nature reserve offers unspoiled terrain for those willing to hike.
valueDescription: Day lift passes are priced at €59 for adults, €50 for youth, and €30 for children (2025/26 season). Family discounts and multi-day passes are available. The resort is part of the Schneebärenland tariff association, allowing access to multiple resorts (Tauplitz, Planneralm, Riesneralm) with one pass. Accommodation in Altaussee offers good value compared to larger Austrian resorts, with free parking at the base station.
familyDescription: The children's ski area at Sandling features a 'Megastar' platter lift, two magic carpets (short and long), and a ski carousel, providing a safe environment for first turns. The 9 km Loser Panorama slope is Austria's longest family run, suitable for progressing beginners. Two ski schools (Skischule Haim and Ski- & Snowboardschule Loitzl) offer English-speaking instruction. The entire ski area can be explored on easy slopes, and a practice rope tow near the Loseralm at the Panoramabahn mountain station completes the beginner offering.
apresSkiDescription: The resort has one bar and no clubs. Après-ski is relaxed, centered around mountain huts serving traditional Austrian dishes like Wiener Schnitzel and Germknödel. The Loseralm and Loserhütte offer terrace dining with mountain views. The atmosphere is calm rather than rowdy, with early evening dining and hot drinks.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is based in the traditional village of Altaussee, with authentic Austrian architecture and a relaxed, unspoiled atmosphere. The surrounding area is a nature reserve (Loser Plateau), offering pristine alpine scenery. The ski area is on the Loser mountain, accessed via a scenic toll road (Loser Panoramastraße). The village has a charming, non-commercial feel, with a lake (Altausseer See) providing a picturesque backdrop.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes 7 lifts: 1 gondola, 1 high-speed six-seater chairlift, 1 quad chairlift, and 4 surface lifts. In 2024, a new 10-seater panoramic gondola (Loser Panorama Gondola) replaced the Loser-Jet 6-seater chairlift and the Loser 2 double chairlift, connecting the base station, mid-station at Augstalm, and summit station at Loser Alm. Lifts are equipped with hoods and heated seats for comfort. Wait times are short due to modern infrastructure.
linkedResortsDescription: Loser-Altaussee is part of the Schneebärenland tariff association, which includes Tauplitz, Planneralm, and Riesneralm. A single ski pass grants access to these resorts. The wider Ausseerland/Salzkammergut region also offers over 200 km of cross-country trails and other winter activities. Nearby ski areas include Dachstein Krippenstein and Tauplitzalm.

terrainDescription: The terrain on the Niedere is predominantly gentle and wide, making it well-suited for beginners and families. There is one steep black run for more advanced skiers. The area also features 3 km of ski routes for off-piste exploration. The slopes are well-groomed and the layout is compact, with most runs accessible from the top of the gondola.
offPisteDescription: The Niedere is known for its deep snow slopes (Tiefschneehänge) off the groomed pistes, offering good freeriding opportunities. When snow conditions allow, attractive firn runs on the Bezau side are popular. The area is also a starting point for ski tours in the Niedere-Winterstaude region. The Baumgarten mountain station is a common access point for off-piste and ski touring.
valueDescription: Lift pass prices are low compared to larger Austrian resorts. A day ticket for adults costs €38 (main season), with youth at €34.50 and children at €21.50. The resort is part of the 3TälerPass (3Valley ski pass), which provides access to 32 ski areas in the region for a single pass, offering good value for those wanting to explore multiple areas.
familyDescription: The Niedere ski area is explicitly family-friendly, with a dedicated children's area at the valley station in Andelsbuch featuring a practice lift and a magic carpet (Zauberteppich) on a gentle ski meadow. There is also a ski kindergarten and a children's restaurant. The slopes are mostly easy and the area is compact, making it easy for parents to keep an eye on children. The gondola from Bezau provides quick access to the mountain.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski area is located on the Niedere Höhe mountain in the Bregenzerwald, a traditional Austrian region. The base village Bezau is a small Tyrolean town with a cable car station. The area offers scenic views across the Bregenzerwald and as far as Lake Constance on clear days. The mountain station has a panorama restaurant. The resort is not purpose-built; it retains a natural alpine character with traditional architecture in the villages.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of a 60-person gondola from Bezau to the Baumgarten mountain station, covering 930 vertical meters in a few minutes. On the mountain, there are two T-bar lifts (drag lifts) serving the slopes. The gondola operates on a 30-minute interval schedule. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, but the area is small with limited capacity.
linkedResortsDescription: The Niedere ski area is part of the 3TälerPass (3Valley ski pass) network, which grants access to 32 ski areas in the Bregenzerwald, Großes Walsertal, Lechtal, and Balderschwang (Allgäu). The pass also covers ski bus transport between areas. Additionally, the Niedere area is connected to the Winterstaude area for ski tours, and the Baumgarten mountain station serves as a starting point for tours into the wider region.

terrainDescription: The ski area is located on the east-facing slopes of Hochalm, with tree-free upper sections offering an open feel and plenty of sunshine. The terrain consists of 32.5 km of pistes, predominantly blue and red runs suitable for beginner and intermediate skiers, with a few black runs including a challenging descent from the main gondola to the valley. The longest run is the 10 km Gipfelbahn-Rauris descent with a vertical drop of 1,235 m. The slopes are well-groomed and the upper area is largely treeless, providing panoramic views of the Hohe Tauern National Park. The area also includes a terrain park (Nugget Park) with rails and obstacles.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs approximately €60-65 for adults, with a 3-day pass from £166. Children's tickets are around €30 per day, and youth tickets €47.50. From 28 March to 6 April 2026, children up to age 15 ski for free when accompanied by an adult holding a pass for at least three days. The resort offers family discounts and is described as having fair prices. Accommodation includes self-catering apartments and hotels, with 3,200 guest beds in the valley. The ski area is one of Austria's first climate-neutral resorts, which may reduce environmental costs.
familyDescription: The resort is specifically designed for families, with a dedicated family slope and a practice lift that is free for children. Two ski schools (Adventure Rauris and Skischule Maier) offer lessons for all ages. There is a family fun slope, a small family park (Nugget Park), and a 2.5 km floodlit toboggan run that is also open for night tobogganing twice a week. The valley station has a children's play area and two huts serving affordable food. The village is walkable and traffic-free, and free ski buses connect the accommodation to the lifts. The resort also offers guided snowshoe tours and winter hiking trails.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is quiet and family-oriented, with 2 bars and pubs and 17 restaurants in the village. The scene is relaxed rather than rowdy, with traditional Austrian hospitality. Visitors recommend the resort for those who want to improve skiing rather than party. There are some nice restaurants and bars, but the nightlife is subdued.
resortCharacterDescription: Rauris is a traditional Austrian village located in a dead-end valley within the Hohe Tauern National Park. It has 3,100 inhabitants and 3,200 guest beds. The village is charming and authentic, with supermarkets, hotels, rental shops, and a few restaurants and bars. The ski area is modern with gondola lifts, but the village retains a quiet, traditional character away from mass tourism. The resort is one of Austria's first climate-neutral ski resorts, with a hydroelectric power plant and sustainable practices.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of 10 lifts, including 3 gondolas (Gipfelbahn, Waldalmbahn, Kreuzbodenbahn), 1 chairlift, and 6 surface lifts. The gondolas provide fast access to the upper slopes at 2,175 m. The lifts are modern and efficient, with chip card technology for ski passes. Queues are rare due to the resort's small size and low crowds. A free ski bus operates within the valley. The system is well-suited for a compact area, with the main gondola serving most of the terrain.

terrainDescription: The ski area features gentle, rolling hills with predominantly easy and intermediate runs. The slopes are wide and well-groomed, with a dedicated children's area called 'Schetti's Kids' Paradise' that includes a ski carousel, magic carpet, igloo, climbing hill, and tubing course. Runs are named after animals with mascots indicating required skill levels, from snowplough turns on the hedgehog circuit to dynamic carving on the lynx piste. The terrain is sheltered and ideal for beginners and families.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable, with a day pass for adults at €36 and children at €20. Family discounts of up to 20% are available. The resort is known for its good value compared to larger Austrian resorts, with budget-friendly accommodation and dining options in the village of Egg.
familyDescription: The resort is specifically designed for families, with a dedicated children's area called 'Schetti's Kids' Paradise' featuring a ski carousel, magic carpet, igloo, climbing hill, and tubing course. The ski school offers lessons for children, and there is free child-minding during the Austrian semester break in February. All slopes have mascots that describe the required skill level, making it easy for parents to choose appropriate runs. The village of Egg has a childcare center and family-friendly amenities.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski is low-key, with a few restaurants and bars in the village of Egg. On the mountain, the Lifthus self-service restaurant at the valley station, the Gasthaus s'Amagmach, and the Teehütte hut provide dining options. The resort has 13 restaurants and 1 bar/café in the area, offering a relaxed atmosphere rather than a party scene.
resortCharacterDescription: Schetteregg is a small, tranquil ski area located in a side valley of the Bregenzerwald. The village of Egg has traditional Austrian charm with cottages and a relaxed atmosphere. The ski area is not a large purpose-built resort but rather a local, family-friendly destination with a focus on gentle skiing and winter activities such as winter hiking, cross-country skiing, and tobogganing.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 6 lifts, including a chairlift and surface lifts, plus a children's carousel and magic carpets in the dedicated kids' area. The lifts are efficient for the size of the area, with minimal queues. The lift system is well-maintained and suitable for beginners and intermediates.
linkedResortsDescription: Schetteregg is part of the 3Valley ski pass, which connects 38 ski areas in the Bregenzerwald, Großes Walsertal, Lechtal, and Allgäu regions. The pass includes free bus travel between resorts, allowing access to a much larger ski area.

terrainDescription: The terrain at Söllereck is dominated by wide, gentle blue runs, with over half the slopes classified as easy. Intermediate red slopes include the Höllwies piste, which was once a FIS race course and offers more challenging gradients. The slopes are set on a partly wooded, grassy mountain, with the intermediate runs towards Ziegelbachhütte providing views of Oberstdorf. A dedicated practice area near the mid-station features magic carpets and a children's play area.
valueDescription: A day lift pass in the main season costs €56.90 for adults, €48 for youth, and €29 for children. Parking at the valley station is free, with around 575 spaces available. The resort is part of the Oberstdorf Kleinwalsertal ski region, where a 6-day pass for the wider area is €342.80 for adults in low season. Self-catering accommodation in nearby Oberstdorf offers budget-friendly options, and the resort's focus on beginners means cheaper beginner-only passes are available.
familyDescription: Söllereck is marketed as a family-friendly ski area and is the home mountain of the Oberstdorf ski schools. Children have a dedicated area called 'Söllis Winterwelt' near the mid-station, with magic carpets, a practice area, and a giant wooden piste basher for climbing and playing. The Allgäu Coaster, a year-round toboggan run, provides additional family fun. Wide blue slopes and a balanced ratio of blue and red runs make progression easy for beginners. A cheaper beginner ski pass is available for those sticking to the learning area.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski area is located on the slopes of the Söllereck mountain, accessible via a modern 10-person gondola from a large parking lot. There is no resort village at the base; the nearest town is Oberstdorf, 4 km away. The area features a mix of wooded and open slopes, with a family-friendly atmosphere and a focus on beginner and intermediate skiing. The mountain itself is partly wooded and grassy, popular for hiking in summer.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 8 lifts: a 10-person gondola (Söllereckbahn) from the valley to the top station, one chairlift, three drag lifts, and three magic carpets. A new 6-person high-speed detachable chairlift (Wannenbahn) is planned for the 2026/27 season. The traditional Höllwies drag lift was modernized and reopened for the 2025/26 season with a new energy-saving drive. The gondola ride takes less than 8 minutes, and lifts operate from 8:30 to 16:00.
linkedResortsDescription: Söllereck is part of the two-country ski region Oberstdorf Kleinwalsertal, which includes ski areas in Germany and Austria. It is primarily responsible for easy slopes within this region. From the Söllereck valley station, other ski areas such as Fellhorn-Kanzelwand are accessible by bus or car. The wider region offers over 200 km of slopes and is linked by a common ski pass.

terrainDescription: The ski area offers 16 prepared pistes covering 12 km, with a mix of blue, red, and black runs including a FIS downhill course. The slopes are on the sunny side of the Mürztal valley, featuring wide-open pistes and forest trails. The upper area provides steeper terrain, while the lower slopes are gentler. The vertical drop is 386 m from 1,050 m to 1,450 m.
valueDescription: Lift passes are affordable, with a day pass for adults at €36 and a six-day pass at €155. Children's day passes are €19. The resort offers a free magic carpet for beginners. Mountain restaurants serve Austrian specialties at fair prices, and multi-day ski tickets are available at a discount.
familyDescription: The resort is marketed as family-friendly, with a dedicated children's area featuring a free magic carpet at Gamsmugl. The slopes are uncrowded, with gentle learning slopes and professional ski instruction. Forest trails and routes provide a safe environment for children. The resort has a relaxed atmosphere and is listed among the top family ski areas in Styria. Accommodation includes the JUFA Hotel with a swimming pool and slide.
resortCharacterDescription: The resort is set in a natural alpine landscape on the sunny side of the Mürztal valley, with stunning views of the Hohe Veitsch massif. It is a small, family-oriented ski area with a relaxed, authentic Austrian atmosphere, not a large purpose-built resort. The village of Veitsch offers basic amenities and accommodation.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system consists of one quad chairlift, two T-bar lifts, one platter lift, and a free magic carpet for beginners. The lifts are modern and well-maintained, with minimal waiting times even during peak periods. Snowmaking covers the entire ski area, ensuring reliable conditions.

terrainDescription: The Ski Juwel area comprises three main sectors across two valleys. On the Alpbach side, the Wiedersbergerhorn (2,128m) offers 55km of pistes, mostly north- and east-facing, with a large open area of easy red and blue runs between 1,900m and 1,700m above the treeline, and tree-lined descents from 1,850m down to 1,350m. Two top-to-bottom runs drop nearly 1,000m to Inneralpbach and the main lift base. The Auffach sector on the Schatzberg (1,903m) features unusually wide, confidence-boosting pistes above the treeline, mostly north-facing, with one red run (13) dropping over the south side. The Niederau sector is separate, with a steep former FIS race course from the Lanerkopf (1,621m) and long, flat nursery slopes in the meadows. The longest run in the area is 7km from summit to base. Snowmaking covers over 85% of pistes.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is available but not the main draw. Marked ski routes on the piste map offer an introduction to unpisted terrain. After fresh snow, the north-facing slopes above Inneralpbach hold powder longest, and tree skiing through the forests below the main lifts can be excellent. Local guides recommend the Greitalm for tree runs and the Kohlgrube for speed runs, accessed from the Hornbahn 2000. A ski touring route (route 66) from Inneralpbach to the summit of the Wiedersbergerhorn is suitable for first-time tourers, with a 2-3 hour climb and a descent on groomed pistes or powder. The area does not have a freeride reputation like Fieberbrunn, but offers good powder days with a guide.
valueDescription: Lift passes are among the more affordable in Austria. A six-day adult pass costs €288 in low season and €319.50 in high season (2023/24 prices). Children aged 6-15 get a 50% reduction, and under-6s ski free. A one-day pass is €56.50 low season, €62.50 high season. Accommodation is also good value: a junior suite at the four-star superior Boglerhof in Alpbach costs less than a standard room in a three-star hotel in Méribel. The Alpbachtal Seenland Card and Wildschönau Card provide free guided snowshoe walks and free ski bus. Many package deals are available, and the ski school offers competitive rates.
familyDescription: Ski Juwel has won multiple awards as a top family resort, including Skiresort.info Test Winner 2025 for Families and Children. There are two dedicated children's areas: the Juppi-Do Kinderland at the Reitherkogel gondola base in Reith, with obstacle courses, ski trails, and magic carpets; and the Schatzberg Zwergenland at the Schatzbergbahn mid-station, featuring a snowball playground, igloo, ski carousel, three magic carpets, a T-bar lift, and a mini fun park. Ten ski schools operate in the area, with English-speaking instructors. The nursery slopes at the base of the Wiedersbergerhorn gondola are gentle, wide, and separated from faster traffic. The compact village of Alpbach is easy to navigate on foot, and the relaxed pace suits families. Off-slope activities include toboggan runs, ice skating, swimming pools, and the Alpine Coaster.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is low-key and friendly. In Niederau, the Hexenhäusl bar in the village centre is popular with locals and visitors. In Alpbach, a typical après-ski tour starts at Joe's Salettl in Inneralpbach, then moves to the Postalm balcony for Aperol, and finishes at the Jakober. Auffach and Niederau have sociable bars, while Alpbach and Reith are quieter, with cosy hotel lounges. Night skiing is available on the Reither Kogel on Thursdays and Fridays, with 6km of floodlit slopes. The area is not a party destination; those seeking big nightlife should go to Mayrhofen or Ischgl.
resortCharacterDescription: Alpbach has been voted Austria's most beautiful village and has a strict building code since 1953 limiting buildings to three storeys with wooden upper floors, preserving its traditional character of wooden chalets and flower-filled balconies. The village is compact and walkable, with a calm, unhurried atmosphere. The Wildschönau villages (Niederau, Auffach, Oberau) are also traditional, with wooden and stone chalets giving an authentic Tyrolean feel. The area is not purpose-built; it consists of working farming communities. The resort is car-free in the village centre, and the pace is relaxed, focusing on skiing rather than partying.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 45 lifts, including 9 gondolas, 3 high-speed six-seater chairlifts, 4 quad chairs, and 29 surface lifts. The key connection is the Verbindungsbahn gondola, installed in 2012, linking Inneralpbach to the Schatzberg above Auffach, creating a fully integrated ski area. The Wiedersbergerhornbahn and Pöglbahn gondolas serve the Alpbach side, with a new high-speed six-seater chairlift (Hornlift 2000) linking the mid-station at 1,638m to the top at 2,030m. The Schatzbergbahn is a high-speed gondola from Auffach. The Reither Kogel has a two-stage gondola and is open for night skiing. Free ski buses connect the villages to the lifts.
linkedResortsDescription: The Ski Juwel Alpbachtal Wildschönau links two valleys – Alpbachtal and Wildschönau – via a gondola built in 2012 from Inneralpbach to the Schatzberg. The combined area includes four main villages: Alpbach and Reith im Alpbachtal in the Alpbachtal, and Auffach and Niederau in the Wildschönau. The total ski area is 113-145km of pistes (depending on measurement), making it one of the top 10 largest ski areas in Tyrol. The connection allows skiers to explore both sides in a single day, with free ski buses linking the villages.

terrainDescription: The terrain in Dachstein West is dominated by intermediate runs, with 65% of the 51 km of marked trails classified as intermediate (red). The resort has 29% beginner and 6% advanced terrain, with no expert runs. The slopes are spread across three access points—Gosau, Russbach, and Annaberg—connected by a 44 km ski circuit called the Dachstein Tour. The vertical drop is 872 metres from a summit of 1,617 m to a base of 745 m. The terrain is generally gentle to moderate, with the advanced runs concentrated on a few steeper pitches such as the Törleck. The resort sits in the Salzkammergut region where the northern Alps transition into gentler hills, and the average run length is just over 1 km.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing in Dachstein West is limited. The resort has no marked off-piste routes within its core area, and the moderate vertical and exposure restrict natural options. There is one ungroomed slope, the Törleck, which is classified as advanced and can be skied off-piste when conditions allow. A signposted skitouring route exists in Russbach, and the nearby Dachstein Krippenstein area (accessible with a multi-day pass) offers 30 km of off-piste variants with over 1,500 m of vertical descent, but that is separate from Dachstein West itself. For freeriders, the resort allows freeride skiing but does not have designated freeride zones.
valueDescription: Adult day lift tickets at Dachstein West cost €65 (2025/26 season), with junior passes at €47, children at €34, and seniors at €61.70. These prices are in the mid-range for Austrian resorts, roughly 20–30% below major destinations like the Arlberg. Multi-day passes from 1.5 days grant access to 70 lifts across 7 regions, including Dachstein Krippenstein and others, offering good value for those who want variety. Accommodation in the villages of Gosau, Russbach, and Annaberg ranges from guesthouses to family hotels, with self-catering apartments available. The resort is considered good value for families due to the extensive beginner and intermediate terrain and the free children's areas.
familyDescription: Dachstein West is specifically designed for families, with four dedicated children's areas: Fredy-Land at the Hornspitz valley station in Gosau, Zwisi-Land near the Panorama Jet Zwieselalm, Bärencamp in Russbach with mascot Bruno, and Fuxi's Kinderland in Annaberg. These areas feature magic carpets, practice lifts, and gentle slopes, and are free for children up to six years old when accompanied by a parent. The ski school offers English-speaking instructors. The villages are compact and walkable, with ski-in/ski-out accommodation available. A free ski bus connects the three villages and surrounding towns. The resort also has a natural toboggan run in Gosau's Hintertal valley, with free sled rental.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski in Dachstein West is relaxed and low-key. The resort has several traditional mountain huts and piste restaurants serving Austrian cuisine, such as the Ausblick and Sonnenalm huts in Gosau and d'Speis Food Truck at the Hornbahn valley station in Russbach. There is no exuberant nightlife; the atmosphere is more suited to families and those seeking a quiet evening. Some bars and restaurants offer drinks after skiing, but the scene is modest. The resort does not have nightclubs or large party venues.
resortCharacterDescription: Dachstein West is not a purpose-built resort but a collection of traditional Austrian villages—Gosau, Russbach, and Annaberg—in the Salzkammergut region, a UNESCO World Heritage area. Gosau is a ribbon village stretching 14 km along the valley, while Russbach and Annaberg are smaller mountain villages. The architecture is traditional, with wooden chalets and guesthouses. The area is known for its Dachstein panorama and is less commercialised than larger Austrian resorts. The resort has a calm, family-oriented atmosphere, with a focus on outdoor activities rather than après-ski.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system in Dachstein West consists of 24 lifts (as of 2025/26), including three modern gondolas: Hornspitz-Express, Panorama Jet Zwieselalm, and Donnerkogelbahn. Many chairlifts are equipped with weather protection hoods and heated seats. Older T-bar lifts serve as practice and connecting lifts. Two new 6-seater chairlifts (Angeralm and Aussichtsberg) with hoods, heated seats, and automatic locking are being built for the 2025/26 season to improve connections between Gosau and Annaberg. The uphill capacity is 27,600 persons per hour. Lift queues are generally short, even during Austrian school holidays. The resort also offers floodlit skiing every Friday evening on the Hornspitz slope from 18:30 to 20:30.
linkedResortsDescription: With a multi-day ski pass of 1.5 days or more, Dachstein West is linked to seven other ski regions, providing access to over 70 lifts and 160 km of slopes. The linked resorts include Dachstein Krippenstein Obertraun, Feuerkogel Ebensee, Karkogel & Sonnleitenlifte Abtenau, Skilift Lammertal Lungötz, St. Martin am Tennengebirge, Zinkenlifte Bad Dürrnberg, and Werfenweng (with a season pass). The Dachstein Tour, a 44 km ski circuit, connects the three base villages of Gosau, Russbach, and Annaberg within the core area. The nearby Dachstein Krippenstein is particularly noted for its freeride terrain and a long 11 km downhill run to Obertraun.

terrainDescription: The SkiWelt Wilder Kaiser-Brixental ski area is one of the largest interconnected ski areas in Austria, spanning nine villages: Söll, Scheffau, Ellmau, Going, Brixen im Thale, Westendorf, Hopfgarten, Itter, and Kelchsau. The slope network is well-balanced, with wide family runs, challenging red descents, and demanding black sections, plus 27 km of marked ski routes for experienced skiers. The most prominent peak, Hohe Salve (1,829 m), offers challenging on- and off-piste runs. Long on-piste descents exceed 1,200 m vertical from Hohe Salve into Hopfgarten (620 m) and over 1,000 m from Choralpe (1,820 m) into Westendorf (802 m). The black run from Choralpe has a maximum gradient of 80%. The area also includes three freestyle fun parks, race courses, and night skiing.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste terrain is limited compared to the groomed runs, but the Westendorf side offers good tree runs on powder days. The area includes 27 km of marked ski routes that provide off-piste-like experiences within the ski area. The Hohe Salve peak attracts advanced skiers with challenging off-piste lines. Overall, off-piste quality is not the resort's strongest feature, with limited advanced off-piste terrain available.
valueDescription: A day lift pass in main season costs €76 for adults, with youth and children tickets at €57 and €38 respectively. The SkiWelt is considered good value compared to larger Austrian resorts, with a value-for-money rating of 4.59/5 from user reviews. The Söll village in particular is noted for strong value relative to its access to the vast SkiWelt area. Multi-day passes and family packages offer further savings.
familyDescription: The SkiWelt has 22 ski schools with dedicated children's areas, offering courses from age two and childcare from five months (or from one year according to some sources). Free practice lifts are available in the valley for beginners. Special children's zones include the Igloo Village and natural ice sun lounge at the top of the Hochbrixen cable car. The area scores 4.89/5 for family friendliness in user reviews, with wide, gentle blue runs and reliable lift systems.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is lively but not overwhelming, with a rating of 4.27/5. Notable venues include the Moonlightbar in Söll, a popular spot with DJ sets and a fun atmosphere, and the Brixner Stadl in Brixen im Thale, known for its lively scene and drinks. Over 80 mountain huts and inns across the villages provide a range of options from quiet to energetic. The resort also has Austria's largest night skiing area in Söll with 10 km of illuminated slopes.
resortCharacterDescription: The SkiWelt consists of nine authentic Tyrolean villages—Brixen im Thale, Ellmau, Going, Hopfgarten, Itter, Kelchsau, Scheffau, Söll, and Westendorf—each with distinct charm and family-run accommodations. The villages are not purpose-built but traditional, with the highest density of mountain huts and inns in Austria (over 80). The area offers ski-in/ski-out access and a car-free experience in many parts, with free shuttle buses between villages.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises 81 lifts: 16 gondolas, 1 funicular, 32 chairlifts, and various surface lifts. The network is modern and efficient, with high transport capacity and short waiting times. The SkiWelt features the world's first 8-seater chairlift with an integrated photovoltaic system, and many lifts run on green electricity from local hydropower. Lift efficiency is rated 4.57/5 by users.
linkedResortsDescription: The SkiWelt is connected to the neighboring KitzSki ski region around Kitzbühel via the KitzSkiWelt Tour, which is the world's longest ski circuit, encompassing approximately 88 km of descents and 17,000 m of elevation gain. This connection gives access to a total of 284 miles (457 km) of slopes. The SkiWelt villages are all interconnected by lifts and pistes, allowing skiers to traverse the entire area without repeating runs. A marked SkiWelt Tour circuit covers 69 km clockwise or 84 km counter-clockwise through all villages.

terrainDescription: The terrain across Flachau, Wagrain, and St. Johann-Alpendorf ranges from gentle beginner slopes to challenging black runs. Flachau is known for its wide, sun-drenched pistes and hosts the Hermann Maier World Cup course. Wagrain features a FunSlope with over 30 elements and the SOKO Funslope. Alpendorf offers the Trio Infernale – three steep black runs – and a 1 km snow park with kickers and rails. The 12 Peaks Tour connects 12 peaks and 5 valleys for advanced skiers. Children have dedicated areas like the Devil's Route adventure descent and Wagraini's Winter World. The area also includes snow parks, race tracks, and SkiMovie tracks for timed runs.
valueDescription: Ski pass holders can travel for free on all public transport in the Salzburg region on the day of arrival and departure, including trains and buses. Free ski buses connect the villages of Flachau, Wagrain, and St. Johann. Accommodation guests receive a Mobility Card for free public transport throughout Salzburg. E-charging stations are available at valley stations for electric cars.
familyDescription: Wagraini's Winter World on Grafenberg is a children's paradise with practice slopes, a tubing track, tire carousel, and a heated dwarf house. The Fichtelland adventure zone offers playful trails and magic carpets. The Devil's Route is an adventure descent with characters like Krampus, witches, and ghosts. Eleven ski schools operate across the area, offering children's courses from age 4 and ski kindergartens from age 2. The Flachau ski school takes children from age 3.5. The Alpendorf snow park has over 30 elements including kickers and rails. Free ski buses make getting around easy, and the villages are compact and walkable.
apresSkiDescription: The après-ski scene is centered around the Dampfkessel (Steam boiler) in Flachau, a legendary spot for drinks and music. Other mountain huts like Birkhahnalm and Franzl Alm offer more relaxed evening dining. The overall atmosphere is moderate rather than rowdy, with lively evenings concentrated in specific venues.
resortCharacterDescription: The three villages of Flachau, Wagrain, and St. Johann-Alpendorf are traditional Austrian alpine settlements with modern ski infrastructure. Flachau has a lively atmosphere, Wagrain is family-friendly, and Alpendorf is peaceful. The villages are connected by free ski buses and offer a mix of traditional architecture and modern amenities. The area is not purpose-built but has grown organically from historic villages.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system is modern and efficient, with nearly all chairlifts being six or eight-seaters equipped with weather bubbles and heated seats. The G-Link is a large aerial tram carrying 160 people across the valley between Wagrain and Flachau. Other notable lifts include the Achterjet gondola in Flachau and the Panorama Link connecting to Flachauwinkl. The Flying Mozart gondola in Wagrain has themed cabins. Wait times are minimal due to the high capacity.
linkedResortsDescription: Snow Space Salzburg is part of the Ski amadé network, which totals 760 km of pistes. The area is connected to Shuttleberg Flachauwinkl-Kleinarl and Skiparadies Zauchensee via the Panorama Link gondola, giving access to a combined 210 km of slopes. The 12 Peaks Tour is a ski circuit that crosses 12 peaks and 5 valleys within the wider area.

terrainDescription: Spiazzi di Gromo offers 7 to 8 runs totaling 15–18 km of skiable terrain, with a vertical drop of 600 m from 1,200 m to 1,800 m. The slopes are divided into one easy blue run at the base, five intermediate red runs, and two challenging black runs descending from Monte Corru. The black runs are long and steep, hosting international competitions. The terrain is set in the Orobie Alps, surrounded by dense forests, with a mix of gentle slopes for beginners and more demanding pistes for advanced skiers. The compact layout makes navigation straightforward.
offPisteDescription: Off-piste skiing is not a primary focus at Spiazzi di Gromo, but ski touring routes are available along Monte Timogno and Colle Vaccarizza. The resort's location within the Orobie Alps natural park provides opportunities for backcountry exploration, though no specific off-piste runs are marked or described in detail. Snowshoeing is also popular on the edges of the pistes.
valueDescription: Spiazzi di Gromo is one of the most economical ski resorts in Lombardy, with a day lift pass costing €35 on holidays and a six-day pass at €125 for adults (€69 for children). The resort is known for being budget-friendly, especially for day trips from Milan and Bergamo. Accommodation options include affordable hotels, apartments, and agriturismi. The overall cost is low compared to larger Alpine resorts, making it a good value for families and groups.
familyDescription: The resort is family-friendly with a dedicated beginner area featuring a blue run at the base served by a magic carpet. The ski school (Scuola Sci Spiazzi) is highly rated, especially for school groups. Off-slope activities include a 1,000 m bobsleigh track, a natural ice skating rink, and a sledding run for children. However, the resort lacks a dedicated snow park and children's play area, as noted in some reviews. The compact village layout makes it easy to keep track of children.
apresSkiDescription: Après-ski is relaxed and low-key, centered around the Rifugio Vodala at 1,750 m, which can host up to 300 people and offers typical Lombard cuisine. In the evenings, the rifugio hosts singing and dancing, popular with younger visitors. A few local bars and restaurants in the village serve local dishes, but the scene is quiet rather than lively.
resortCharacterDescription: Spiazzi di Gromo is a small ski resort that has grown organically at the edge of the medieval village of Gromo, in the Val Seriana. The resort consists of a new nucleus of cottages and cozy hotels set in a dense forest, contrasting with the ancient stone buildings of the original village. It is not a purpose-built station but a natural expansion of an existing settlement, offering a picturesque setting in the Orobie Alps.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system comprises five lifts: four chairlifts (one triple and three double chairs) and one surface lift (magic carpet). There are no gondolas or cable cars. The lifts have an hourly capacity of 5,000 people and serve the slopes from the base at 1,200 m to the top at 1,800 m. The system is adequate for the resort's size but not high-speed or modern.

terrainDescription: The ski area at Col de Rousset consists of 26 runs spread across an elevation from 1,255 m to 1,700 m. The terrain is dominated by beginner and intermediate runs, with 46% beginner, 29% intermediate, 18% advanced, and 7% expert according to OnTheSnow. There are two dedicated beginner areas: one in the heart of the resort with small green runs served by a carpet, a snow line, and a small ski lift, and another on the Beure plateau with a second snow line. The resort also features a snowpark and a boardercross for freestyle enthusiasts. The longest run is 1.3 km, and total skiable terrain is 22 km. The slopes are set between forests and cliffs, offering panoramic views over the Diois valley.
valueDescription: Col de Rousset offers good value for a small family resort. A family pass costs €79.60 per day for up to four people (2 adults and 2 children under 18, or 1 adult and 3 children), with additional children at €13 each. Daily adult lift tickets are around €18 on weekdays and weekends, with half-day tickets at €16. A six-day adult pass is €115.90. The resort also offers a snow shuttle from Die for €9 round trip, making it accessible without a car. Season passes (€231 for adults) also grant access to the nearby resorts of Font d'Urle and Lus-la-Jarjatte. Children under 5 ski free.
familyDescription: Col de Rousset is family-friendly with several dedicated facilities. The ESF (French Ski School) offers a Kindergarten for children aged 6-9 and a Piou-Piou initiation club for ages 3-5. There are two beginner areas with carpets and snow lines. Non-ski activities include a rail sled, tubing, dog-sledding, and sledging runs. The resort provides off-bag rooms with microwaves and toilets, one in the heart of the resort and one at the Beure car park. A snow shuttle from Die (€9 round trip) makes access easier for families without a car. Children under 5 ski for free, and the family pass allows four people to ski together at a reduced rate.
apresSkiDescription: Apres-ski at Col de Rousset is low-key and centered on the resort's dining options. The resort has bars and restaurants serving sweet and savoury crepes, traditional dishes like gratin de ravioles and tartiflette, and hot and cold drinks. At the Beure car park, L'échappée Belle offers ravioles, daily specials, soups, and waffles. A snack bar and bread store operate within the resort. For more variety, the village of Saint-Agnan-en-Vercors (8 km away) has a bakery, bar, and restaurant, while La Chapelle en Vercors or Die (30 minutes by car) offer additional restaurants, bars, and amenities. There is no mention of nightclubs or lively bars.
resortCharacterDescription: Col de Rousset is a small, family-oriented resort situated at a mountain pass in the Vercors High Plateaux Nature Reserve. The resort is set between forests and cliffs, with panoramic views over the Diois valley. It was developed on former communal pastures and has a functional layout with ski-in/ski-out access from accommodation. The resort consists of a central area with shops, restaurants, and lift bases, plus an upper car park at Beure. The architecture is not described in detail, but the setting is natural rather than purpose-built in a traditional alpine style.
liftSystemDescription: The resort has 11 lifts: one high-speed detachable quad chairlift and 10 surface lifts (including 6 téléskis and 1 téléski école). The chairlift provides access to the main slopes, while surface lifts serve beginner areas and upper sectors. Lifts operate from 9:00 to 17:00 daily.
linkedResortsDescription: The Col de Rousset season pass also provides access to the nearby resorts of Font d'Urle and Lus-la-Jarjatte, both in the Drôme region. Additionally, the Grande Traversée du Vercors, a 29 km groomed cross-country trail, connects Col de Rousset to Vassieux and Font d'Urle, linking the resorts for Nordic skiing.

terrainDescription: The Steinplatte Winklmoosalm ski area sits on a sunny high plateau at 1,100–1,900 m. The terrain consists of wide, groomed pistes, with 85% snowmaking coverage. The slopes are predominantly south-facing, providing good visibility and soft snow in the afternoon. The ski area offers 42 km of pistes, with a mix of easy and intermediate runs, and a few difficult runs. The longest run is 9.5 km. The slopes are groomed daily and rarely crowded.
offPisteDescription: The Schwarzlofer Pasture provides deep-snow slopes for freeriders. There are 3 km of marked ski routes and some gladed areas for off-piste skiing. The off-piste terrain is limited but offers opportunities for powder skiing after fresh snowfall.
valueDescription: A day lift pass costs €66 (2025/26 season). On Super Tuesdays in January and from 10 March 2026, the day ticket is reduced to €41. The ski area is part of the 3-Country Leisure Arena, SuperSkiCard, and Snow Card Tirol networks, offering multi-resort options. Ski rental for 6 days starts at around 750 DKK (approx. €100). The mountain huts offer fair prices for food and drink.
familyDescription: The Steinplatte is a certified Tyrolean family ski region. The Triassic Kinderland at the mountain station offers childcare for children aged 3 and above, with half-day and full-day options. The Hausberg Arena in Waidring provides a free beginner area for children up to age 6. The Triassic Funline features dinosaur-themed elements and a wave track. The Triassic Park includes a 1 km winter hiking trail, indoor playrooms, and a Mama Lounge for warming up. The ski school in Waidring offers lessons for children. The resort is car-free in the village and has a free shuttle bus.
resortCharacterDescription: The ski area is accessed from the village of Waidring, a traditional Tyrolean village, and from Reit im Winkl in Germany. The Steinplatte plateau itself is a high-altitude sunny plateau with modern lift infrastructure. The base villages retain alpine charm with traditional architecture. The resort is purpose-built for skiing with gondolas and chairlifts, but the villages offer a relaxed, family-friendly atmosphere.
liftSystemDescription: The lift system includes a modern 15-person gondola from Waidring and an 8-seater gondola from Seegatterl. The new Bäreckbahn is a detachable 6-seater chairlift from Doppelmayr with heated seats, weather protection hoods, and automatic bar locking. There are 14 lifts in total, including chairlifts and surface lifts. The lifts operate from 08:30 to 16:30, with gondolas until 17:00. Queues are rare outside peak weeks.
linkedResortsDescription: The Steinplatte ski area is linked with the German Winklmoosalm ski area, accessible with one ski pass.
